topics.title: Basic Differences Between Conventional and High-Performance Crafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST745 - DESIGN OF HIGH SPEED CRAFTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Differences Between Conventional and High-Performance Crafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Conventional Craft** (also termed a *displacement hull vessel*) is defined under KTU naval architecture terminology as a marine vehicle whose entire weight is statically supported by the **buoyancy force** of the displaced water (Archimedes' Principle), with the hull operating entirely below the free water surface at all times. The Froude Number ($F_n$) for such vessels is conventionally less than **0.40**, placing them in the sub-critical displacement regime.

A **High-Performance Craft (HPC)** is defined as a marine vessel that uses one or more **dynamic lift-generating mechanisms** (planing, hydrofoiling, air cushion, semi-submerged hulls, or surface-piercing) to partially or fully support its weight, enabling operation at Froude Numbers greater than **0.40**, often exceeding **1.0** (super-critical regime).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental discriminator between the two craft categories is the **mode of weight support** — buoyancy (static) versus dynamic lift (hydrodynamic/aerodynamic). This single distinction cascades into every downstream design parameter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy for Intuition

Think of a **conventional craft** like a **regular cork floating in a bathtub** — push it forward, and it simply slides through the water at a leisurely pace, supported entirely by the water it pushes aside. Its maximum practical speed is severely limited by the physics of wave-making.

Now imagine a **high-performance craft** like a **hovercraft at a theme park ride** — instead of just floating, it actively generates lift through speed (planing) or pressurized air (air cushion) to rise up and skim across the surface. It "cheats" the wave-making barrier by either lifting out of the water or riding on a cushion of air.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Table:**
>
> | Conventional Craft | High-Performance Craft |
> |---|---|
> | Elephant walking through a swamp (heavy, slow, supported by ground) | Cheetah sprinting across the savannah (light, dynamic, lift-assisted) |
> | Family sedan on a highway (limited to ~120 km/h) | Formula-1 race car (aerodynamic downforce unlocks 300+ km/h) |
> | Walking in a swimming pool (water resistance dominates) | Hydrofoil board gliding above the water (lift removes drag source) |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The primary non-dimensional metric used in KTU textbooks to classify craft speed regimes is:

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$

where $V$ is the vessel speed in m/s, $g$ is gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**), and $L$ is the waterline length in meters.

- **Speed-Length Ratio (SLR):** $SLR = \frac{V}{\sqrt{L}}$ (in knots/√ft, or m/s/√m depending on the system used).
- **Volumetric Froude Number:** Used for air-cushion vehicles where the cushion length is the characteristic dimension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Speed Threshold:** The hull speed barrier for a conventional displacement craft is approximately $V_{critical} \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{wl}}$ (in knots, with $L$ in feet). Beyond this, the wave-making resistance grows exponentially, demanding disproportionately large engine power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification Logic — Step by Step

The categorization of a marine craft depends on **how its weight is supported** and **how it interacts with the free surface**. KTU 2024 Scheme classifies craft into the following hierarchy:

### Step 1: Determine the Speed Regime
- Calculate $F_n$ using waterline length.
- If $F_n < 0.40$ → **Displacement (Conventional)**.
- If $0.40 \leq F_n \leq 1.0$ → **Semi-Displacement / Planing**.
- If $F_n > 1.0$ → **High-Speed / Supersonic regime**.

### Step 2: Identify the Lift Mechanism
- **Static Lift (Buoyancy):** Weight = $\rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ (Archimedes).
- **Dynamic Lift:** Weight = $C_L \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \cdot S$ (planing/hydrofoil lift equation).
- **Aerostatic Lift:** Weight = $(P_{cushion} - P_{atm}) \cdot A_{cushion}$ (ACV air cushion).

### Step 3: Determine Resistance Behavior
- **Conventional:** Dominated by frictional + wave-making resistance; rises as $V^6$ near hull speed.
- **High-Performance:** Wave-making suppressed; resistance dominated by viscous + aerodynamic drag.

### Step 4: Power Demand Curve
- **Conventional:** Power grows as $V^7$ (EHP) — exponential climb near critical speed.
- **High-Performance:** Power grows approximately as $V^3$ to $V^4$ — much more favorable scaling.

### Step 5: Structural & Stability Implications
- **Conventional:** Long, slender hulls; high metacentric height (GM); transverse stability is paramount.
- **High-Performance:** Wide, flat, or foil-borne hulls; longitudinal (porpoising) stability dominates; transverse stability often sacrificed for lift.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude Number (dimensionless) | — |
| 2 | $SLR = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{L_{wl}}}$ | Speed-Length Ratio (knots / √ft) | kn/√ft |
| 3 | $R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{APP}$ | Total Resistance = Frictional + Wave + Appendage | N (or kN) |
| 4 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC Frictional Coefficient (1957) | — |
| 5 | $R_n = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number | — |
| 6 | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ | Effective Horsepower (metric) | HP (metric) |
| 7 | $SHP = \dfrac{EHP}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_S}$ | Shaft Horsepower | HP |
| 8 | $\Delta = \rho g \nabla$ | Displacement (static buoyancy) | N |
| 9 | $L_{dynamic} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$ | Dynamic lift (planing/foil) | N |
| 10 | $V_{hull} \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{ft}}$ | Hull speed limit (conventional) | knots |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by naval architects at shipyards such as **Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE Kolkata, and L&T Shipbuilding** to perform preliminary resistance estimates, contract bidding, and concept-stage feasibility studies. High-performance craft analysis is also critical for **INS Vikrant-class design reviews** and **fast attack craft (FAC) procurement** by the Indian Navy.

## 2.3 Comparison Matrix: Conventional vs. High-Performance

| Parameter | Conventional Craft | High-Performance Craft |
|---|---|---|
| Weight Support | Pure Buoyancy (100%) | Buoyancy + Dynamic Lift |
| Froude Number | $F_n < 0.40$ | $F_n > 0.40$ (often $> 1.0$) |
| Speed Range | Up to ~25 knots | 30–60+ knots |
| Hull Form | Displacement, slender | Planing, foil, ACV, SWATH |
| Resistance Behavior | $R \propto V^6$ near hull speed | $R \propto V^{3}$ to $V^4$ |
| Power Demand | $EHP \propto V^7$ | $EHP \propto V^3$–$V^4$ |
| Hull Length/Beam Ratio (L/B) | High (5–7) | Low (2–4 for planing) |
| Stability Criterion | Transverse GM dominant | Longitudinal (porpoising) dominant |
| Payload Capacity | High | Moderate |
| Fuel Efficiency at Cruise | Good at low speeds | Good at high speeds |
| Examples | Cargo ships, tankers, trawlers | Hydrofoils, ACVs, FFACs, racing yachts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Froude Number (from Dimensional Analysis)

The Froude Number is derived using the **Buckingham Pi Theorem** by selecting governing variables for ship resistance in a gravity-dominated free-surface flow.

### Step 1: List the Governing Variables

The total resistance $R$ of a ship depends on:
- Fluid density $\rho$ [kg/m³]
- Velocity $V$ [m/s]
- Characteristic length $L$ [m]
- Gravitational acceleration $g$ [m/s²]
- Fluid kinematic viscosity $\nu$ [m²/s]

So we have 6 variables in 3 primary dimensions (M, L, T).

### Step 2: Apply Buckingham Pi Theorem

Number of $\Pi$ groups = 6 variables − 3 dimensions = **3 dimensionless groups**.

Selecting $\rho$, $V$, $L$ as repeating variables:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{R}{\rho V^2 L^2} \quad \text{(Resistance Coefficient)}$$

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} \quad \text{(Froude Number)}$$

$$\Pi_3 = \frac{VL}{\nu} \quad \text{(Reynolds Number)}$$

### Step 3: Final Functional Relationship

$$\frac{R}{\rho V^2 L^2} = f\left( \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}, \frac{VL}{\nu} \right)$$

For full-scale ships, the Reynolds number effect is small (turbulent boundary layer fully developed), so the dependence on $\Pi_3$ is weak. This gives:

$$\frac{R}{\rho V^2 L^2} \approx f(F_n)$$

### Step 4: Verify Dimensional Homogeneity

Check $F_n$:

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} = \frac{[\text{m/s}]}{\sqrt{[\text{m/s}^2][\text{m}]}} = \frac{[\text{m/s}]}{[\text{m/s}]} = \text{dimensionless} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The Froude Number physically represents the **ratio of inertial forces to gravitational forces** in the flow around a free surface. When $F_n < 1$, gravity waves propagate faster than the ship; when $F_n > 1$, the ship outruns its own wave system — the **supercritical regime**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Hull Speed Limit (Conventional Craft)

For a displacement hull, the vessel generates a **transverse wave system**. The phase velocity of a gravity wave of wavelength $\lambda$ in deep water is:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{g\lambda}{2\pi}}$$

### Step 1: Relate Wave Length to Ship Length

The dominant transverse wave generated by a ship has wavelength approximately equal to the waterline length:

$$\lambda \approx L_{wl}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Phase Velocity

$$V_{wave} = \sqrt{\frac{g L_{wl}}{2\pi}}$$

### Step 3: Critical Speed Condition

The hull speed is reached when the ship speed equals the wave phase velocity. The bow wave and stern wave then constructively interfere at the stern, creating the **"hump"** in the resistance curve:

$$V_{critical} = \sqrt{\frac{g L_{wl}}{2\pi}}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate for Typical Units (feet and knots)

Using $g = 32.2 \text{ ft/s}^2$ and converting to knots (1 knot = 1.689 ft/s):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{critical} &= \sqrt{\frac{32.2 \cdot L_{ft}}{2\pi}} \text{ ft/s} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{32.2 \cdot L_{ft}}{6.2832}} \\
&= \sqrt{5.126 \cdot L_{ft}} \text{ ft/s} \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{5.126 \cdot L_{ft}}}{1.689} \text{ knots} \\
&= 1.34 \sqrt{L_{ft}} \text{ knots}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Once a conventional craft reaches $1.34\sqrt{L_{ft}}$ knots, it sits in a "trough" between its bow and stern waves. To go faster, it must climb up the back of its own wave — requiring **infinite power** in the ideal inviscid limit. This is the fundamental physical barrier that **high-performance crafts bypass** by lifting out of the water.

## 3.3 Derivation of Dynamic Lift (Planing Hull)

For a planing craft, the dynamic lift at small angle of attack $\tau$ is given by the **Savitsky formula** (1964):

$$L_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 b^2 C_{L\beta}$$

where:
- $b$ = beam (chord) of the planing surface [m]
- $C_{L\beta}$ = lift coefficient based on deadrise angle $\beta$

For a flat plate at small angle of attack:

$$C_{L\beta} \approx \tau \left(0.0120 \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{b}} + \frac{0.0055 \lambda^{5/2}}{C_V^{1/2} \cdot b}\right)$$

where $\lambda$ is the wetted length and $C_V = V/\sqrt{gb}$ is the volumetric Froude number.

### Total Resistance at Planing Condition:

$$R_T = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 b^2 C_{D\beta}$$

with $C_{D\beta}$ similarly derived from Savitsky's empirical charts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Notice how the dynamic lift formula is identical in form to the **thin-airfoil (lift = $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$)** used in aeronautics. This is why planing hull design borrows heavily from aircraft wing theory.

## 3.4 Power Scaling Derivation

### Conventional Craft (Displacement)

For a conventional craft near hull speed, wave-making resistance scales as:

$$R_W \propto \Delta \cdot F_n^6$$

EHP scales as:

$$EHP = R_T \cdot V \propto V^7$$

This is the **seventh-power law** — a 10% speed increase requires a **95% increase in power**.

### High-Performance Craft (Planing)

For a planing craft, the dominant resistance term scales as:

$$R_{friction} \propto V^2$$

Therefore:

$$EHP \propto V^3$$

A 10% speed increase requires only a **33% increase in power** — dramatically more efficient at high speed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Craft Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Marine Craft Universe] --> B0{Froude Number}
    B0 -->|Fn less than 0.40| C0[Conventional Displacement Craft]
    B0 -->|Fn between 0.40 and 1.0| D0[Semi-Displacement Craft]
    B0 -->|Fn greater than 1.0| E0[High-Performance Craft]
    C0 --> C1[Cargo Ships]
    C0 --> C2[Tankers]
    C0 --> C3[Fishing Trawlers]
    C0 --> C4[Submarines Submerged]
    D0 --> D1[Trawlers with Bulbous Bow]
    D0 --> D2[Semi-Planing Patrol Boats]
    E0 --> F0{Lift Mechanism}
    F0 -->|Dynamic Hydrodynamic Lift| G0[Planing Hulls]
    F0 -->|Hydrofoils| H0[Foil Borne Craft]
    F0 -->|Air Cushion| I0[Air Cushion Vehicles ACV]
    F0 -->|Submerged Hull| J0[SWATH Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull]
    G0 --> G1[Fast Attack Craft FAC]
    G0 --> G2[Racing Powerboats]
    H0 --> H1[Commercial Hydrofoil]
    H0 --> H2[Military Hydrofoil]
    I0 --> I1[Hovercraft SR.N4]
    I0 --> I2[LCAC Landing Craft Air Cushion]
    J0 --> J1[Stable Research Vessels]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Resistance Composition Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Conventional_Craft
        RC[Total Resistance] --> RC1[Frictional RF about 60 percent]
        RC --> RC2[Wave Making RW about 30 percent]
        RC --> RC2b[Form and Eddy Resistance about 10 percent]
    end
    subgraph HighPerformance_Craft
        RH[Total Resistance] --> RH1[Frictional RF about 40 percent]
        RH --> RH2[Aerodynamic Drag RA about 25 percent]
        RH --> RH3[Spray and Slamming RS about 20 percent]
        RH --> RH4[Wave Making RW about 15 percent reduced]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Power Demand vs. Speed Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Increase Ship Speed] --> S1{Q1 Hull Form}
    S1 -->|Conventional| S2[EHP proportional to V raised to 7]
    S1 -->|High-Performance| S3[EHP proportional to V raised to 3]
    S2 --> S4[Exponential Power Demand]
    S3 --> S5[Linear to Cubic Power Demand]
    S4 --> S6[Hits Hull Speed Barrier at Fn equals 0.40]
    S5 --> S6b[No Practical Speed Barrier until Propulsive Limit]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Speed Regime Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN0[Input Craft Parameters] --> IN1[Vessel Speed V]
    IN0 --> IN2[Waterline Length L]
    IN1 --> CALC0[Compute Fn]
    IN2 --> CALC0
    CALC0 --> DEC0{Fn Threshold}
    DEC0 -->|Below 0.40| DEC1[Displacement Hull Recommended]
    DEC0 -->|Between 0.40 and 1.0| DEC2[Semi-Planing Hull Recommended]
    DEC0 -->|Above 1.0| DEC3[Dynamic Lift Hull Required]
    DEC1 --> OUT0[Conventional Design Path]
    DEC2 --> OUT1[Transitional Design Path]
    DEC3 --> OUT2[High-Performance Design Path]
    OUT0 --> NOTE0[Validate Static Stability GM]
    OUT1 --> NOTE1[Validate Both Stability Modes]
    OUT2 --> NOTE2[Validate Longitudinal and Porpoising Stability]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**"Define a High-Performance Craft. List any four examples of high-performance marine vessels."**

**Model Answer:**
A high-performance craft (HPC) is a marine vessel that achieves high speeds (typically $F_n > 0.40$) by using **dynamic lift mechanisms** (such as planing, hydrofoils, or air cushions) to partially or fully support its weight, rather than relying solely on static buoyancy.

Four examples are:
1. **Planing hull fast attack craft (FAC)**
2. **Hydrofoil passenger ferries** (e.g., Boeing Jetfoil)
3. **Air-cushion vehicles / Hovercraft** (e.g., SR.N4)
4. **SWATH vessels** (Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull)

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Count Discipline:** KTU expects 3-mark answers in **50–70 words**. One definition + four one-word examples suffices.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**"State the significance of the Froude Number in marine craft design. What is the typical Froude Number boundary between conventional and high-performance craft?"**

**Model Answer:**
The Froude Number ($F_n$) is the ratio of **inertial forces to gravitational forces** in a free-surface flow. It governs the wave-making resistance, predicts the **hull speed barrier** for displacement vessels, and serves as the primary scaling parameter for resistance estimation.

The conventional/high-performance boundary occurs at **$F_n = 0.40$**, which corresponds to the **hull speed limit** $V = 1.34\sqrt{L_{ft}}$ knots.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the **basic differences between conventional and high-performance marine craft** in terms of:
(i) Weight support mechanism
(ii) Speed regime (Froude Number)
(iii) Resistance behavior
(iv) Power demand scaling

**(b) [7 Marks]** A conventional displacement fishing trawler has a **waterline length of 30 m**. Calculate:
(i) The Froude Number at a service speed of **12 knots**.
(ii) The hull speed in knots and m/s.
(iii) Comment on whether the vessel is operating below, at, or above hull speed.
(iv) State the power scaling law for this regime.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The four differences are:

| Parameter | Conventional | High-Performance |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Weight Support** | Static buoyancy (Archimedes) | Dynamic lift (planing/foil/air cushion) |
| **(ii) Speed Regime ($F_n$)** | $F_n < 0.40$ | $F_n > 0.40$, often $F_n > 1.0$ |
| **(iii) Resistance** | Wave-making dominates; $R \propto V^6$ | Wave-making suppressed; $R \propto V^3$ |
| **(iv) Power (EHP)** | $EHP \propto V^7$ | $EHP \propto V^3$ |

A textual sketch description: In a **conventional craft**, the hull is fully submerged and pushes water aside, generating a transverse wave system. In a **high-performance planing craft**, at speed the hull rises and only the aft portion (the "run") remains wetted, supported by hydrodynamic lift.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $L_{wl} = 30$ m
- $V = 12$ knots

**Step (i): Convert speed to m/s**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= 12 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 \text{ m/s per knot} \\
&= 6.173 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (ii): Calculate Froude Number**

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} = \frac{6.173}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 30}}$$

$$\sqrt{9.81 \times 30} = \sqrt{294.3} = 17.156 \text{ m/s}$$

$$F_n = \frac{6.173}{17.156} = 0.360$$

**Step (iii): Calculate Hull Speed (using feet-based formula)**

Convert $L$ to feet:

$$L_{ft} = 30 \text{ m} \times 3.281 = 98.43 \text{ ft}$$

$$V_{hull} = 1.34 \sqrt{98.43} = 1.34 \times 9.921 = 13.29 \text{ knots}$$

Convert to m/s:

$$V_{hull} = 13.29 \times 0.5144 = 6.836 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (iv): Comment**

Since $V_{service} = 12$ knots $< V_{hull} = 13.29$ knots, the vessel is operating **below hull speed** in the **sub-critical displacement regime** ($F_n = 0.36 < 0.40$). This confirms it is a conventional displacement craft with no need for dynamic lift.

**Power Scaling:** $EHP \propto V^7$ (7th-power law) — doubling speed requires $2^7 = 128$ times the power.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **Froude Number** using dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi Theorem). List all governing variables and show the resulting dimensionless groups.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A planing hull craft operates at **40 knots** with a waterline length of **15 m** and a beam of **4 m**.
(i) Calculate the Froude Number based on waterline length.
(ii) Calculate the volumetric Froude Number based on beam.
(iii) Determine which craft category (conventional or high-performance) it belongs to and justify.
(iv) State the dominant resistance component for this craft.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Governing variables for ship resistance:**

$R = f(\rho, V, L, g, \nu)$ — 6 variables, 3 dimensions → 3 $\Pi$ groups.

Repeating variables: $\rho, V, L$.

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{R}{\rho V^2 L^2} \quad \text{(Resistance Coefficient)}$$

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} \quad \text{(Froude Number, gravity/inertia ratio)}$$

$$\Pi_3 = \frac{VL}{\nu} \quad \text{(Reynolds Number, inertia/viscosity ratio)}$$

Functional relationship:

$$\frac{R}{\rho V^2 L^2} = \phi(F_n, R_n)$$

For full-scale ships, $R_n$ effect is small, giving $R/\rho V^2 L^2 \approx \phi(F_n)$.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $V = 40$ knots, $L_{wl} = 15$ m, $b = 4$ m

**Step (i): Convert speed**

$$V = 40 \times 0.5144 = 20.576 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (ii): Froude Number based on L**

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} = \frac{20.576}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 15}} = \frac{20.576}{12.133} = 1.696$$

**Step (iii): Volumetric Froude Number based on beam**

$$C_V = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gb}} = \frac{20.576}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 4}} = \frac{20.576}{6.264} = 3.285$$

**Step (iv): Classification**

Since $F_n = 1.696 > 1.0$, the craft is operating in the **super-critical high-performance regime**. It is unambiguously a **high-performance craft**. The planing hull lifts dynamically above the water surface.

**Step (v): Dominant Resistance**

For a planing hull at high speed, the dominant resistance component is **frictional resistance** (viscous skin friction) plus **aerodynamic drag** from the exposed hull above water. Wave-making resistance is significantly reduced because the hull runs above its own wave system.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Students frequently forget to convert knots to m/s before applying the Froude formula. **Always** check units first.
> 2. **Mixing up $L_{wl}$ and $L_{OA}$:** Use only the **waterline length**, not the overall length, for Froude calculations.
> 3. **Hull-speed formula confusion:** The constant $1.34$ is for $L$ in **feet** and $V$ in **knots**. Using SI directly gives $V_{hull} = \sqrt{gL/(2\pi)}$.
> 4. **Skipping the "comment" step:** KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks for physical interpretation (above/below hull speed). Always state the regime explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing Resistance with Power:** Remember $R \propto V^6$ but $EHP \propto V^7$ (one extra factor of $V$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — memorize before every KTU exam:**

- [ ] **Conventional craft** = pure buoyancy support, $F_n < 0.40$.
- [ ] **High-performance craft** = dynamic lift support, $F_n > 0.40$, often $> 1.0$.
- [ ] **Froude Number:** $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ — ratio of inertial to gravitational forces.
- [ ] **Speed-Length Ratio:** $SLR = V / \sqrt{L_{wl}}$ in knots/√ft.
- [ ] **Hull speed limit:** $V_{hull} = 1.34 \sqrt{L_{ft}}$ knots (conventional only).
- [ ] **Wave-making resistance:** Dominant in displacement regime, $R_W \propto F_n^6$.
- [ ] **Power scaling — Conventional:** $EHP \propto V^7$ (seventh-power law).
- [ ] **Power scaling — High-Performance:** $EHP \propto V^3$ (planing) to $V^4$ (foil).
- [ ] **Stability — Conventional:** Transverse GM is the design driver.
- [ ] **Stability — High-Performance:** Longitudinal stability (porpoising) is critical.
- [ ] **Lift equations:** Static = $\rho g \nabla$ (Archimedes); Dynamic = $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S C_L$.
- [ ] **HPC categories:** Planing hulls, Hydrofoils, Air Cushion Vehicles (ACV), SWATH.
- [ ] **Conversion:** 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; 1 m = 3.281 ft.
- [ ] **ITTC 1957 line:** $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ — for frictional resistance estimation.
- [ ] **Indian Navy relevance:** FAC-M (Fast Attack Craft – Missile) by GRSE and ABG Shipyard operate in HPC regime; INS Jalashwa and frigates are conventional displacement.
- [ ] **Savitsky planing formula:** Used for planing hull resistance/lift at high speed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are High Speed Crafts (HSC)?

A **High Speed Craft (HSC)** is formally defined under the **IMO International Code of Safety for High-Speed Craft (HSC Code, 1994/2000)** as a craft capable of maintaining a **maximum speed (V_max)** in metres per second equal to or exceeding:

$$V_{max} \geq 3.7 \nabla^{0.1667}$$

where **∇** is the volume of displacement in cubic metres. This corresponds roughly to vessels operating above **25 to 30 knots** for typical sizes, extending to 40+ knots for military or competition craft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST745 Module 1):** "Special Features" refers to the *distinctive design, hydrodynamic, structural, and regulatory characteristics* that differentiate high speed craft from conventional displacement ships. The emphasis is on understanding **why** ordinary ship design rules fail at Froude numbers above 0.4.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a conventional cargo ship as an **elephant walking through a lake** — it displaces water slowly, with gravity and buoyancy in a gentle balance. Now picture a **cobra striking** or a **stone skipping across water** — that is a high speed craft. The water is no longer the dominant medium; **aerodynamic lift, planing forces, hydrofoil lift, or air cushion effects** take over.

The key conceptual shift is from **displacement-mode operation** (Archimedes' rule dominates) to **dynamic-mode operation** (lift, momentum, and pressure forces dominate).

## 1.3 Special Features – The Defining List

A high speed craft exhibits the following distinctive features, which are the focus of Module 1:

| # | Special Feature | Dominant Regime |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High Froude Number Operation (Fn > 0.4)** | Planing / Foil-borne / Air-borne |
| 2 | **Slamming & Whipping Loads** | Bottom panel design |
| 3 | **Dynamic Instability (Porpoising, Broaching)** | Motion control |
| 4 | **Lightweight Materials (Al, GRP, Composites)** | Structural efficiency |
| 5 | **High Power-to-Displacement Ratio** | Powering |
| 6 | **Non-Standard Propulsors (Waterjets, Surface Piercing)** | Propulsion |
| 7 | **HSC Code Compliance** | Regulatory |
| 8 | **Motion Damping Devices (Interceptors, Tabs, Foils)** | Ride control |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** "Special features" in HSC design are those attributes that emerge *only* when the dynamic pressure (½ρV²) becomes comparable to or greater than the hydrostatic pressure (ρgT) of the vessel.

## 1.4 Froude Number – The Gateway Parameter

The **Froude Number (Fn)** is the most important dimensionless parameter that classifies a craft as "high speed":

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$$

where:
- **V** = speed in m/s
- **g** = 9.81 m/s²
- **L_WL** = waterline length in metres

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold for High Speed Classification:**
> - **Fn < 0.25** → Displacement / Slow
> - **0.25 ≤ Fn < 0.4** → Semi-displacement / Transition
> - **Fn ≥ 0.4** → **Planing / High Speed** ← KTU focus
> - **Fn ≥ 1.0** → Ultra-high speed (hydrofoils, SES)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrodynamic Regimes of High Speed Craft

The "special" nature of HSC emerges from the **transition between four physical regimes** as speed increases. This is the *core "why"* that KTU examiners love to test.

### Regime 1: Pure Displacement (Fn < 0.25)
- Hull pushes through water; **Archimedes buoyancy** is the only support.
- Wave-making resistance dominates.
- Conventional ship rules apply.
- Examples: cargo ships, trawlers.

### Regime 2: Semi-Displacement / Pre-Planing (0.25 ≤ Fn < 0.4)
- Stern squat increases; **wave pattern is asymmetric**.
- **Transom immersion** begins — water flows off the transom.
- Resistance curve **humps** at the so-called "hump speed."
- Examples: fast patrol boats, semi-displacement catamarans.

### Regime 3: Planing (0.4 ≤ Fn < 1.0) ⭐ *Most common HSC regime*
- **Dynamic lift** from hydrodynamic pressure on the planing bottom supports most of the weight.
- **Wetted length (L_w)** is much shorter than waterline length.
- Resistance becomes **proportional to weight / (beam × trim angle)**.
- **Slender hulls with wide transoms** are optimal.
- Examples: offshore racers, military patrol craft, fast ferries.

### Regime 4: Foil-borne / Air-cushion (Fn > 1.0)
- Entire hull (hydrofoils) or supported on an air cushion (SES/ACV).
- Wetted surface area is minimized.
- Resistance drops dramatically once "out of water."
- Examples: Boeing 929, Lockheed SES, US Navy PHM.

## 2.2 Special Structural Features

### 2.2.1 Slamming Loads
When a high speed craft lands on a wave crest, the **bottom panels experience a near-instantaneous hydrodynamic pressure pulse**. This is called **slamming**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slamming pressure (Faltinsen approximation):**
> $$p_{slam} = 0.5 \, \rho \, V_n^2 \, \pi \, \lambda$$
> where **V_n** is the normal velocity of the hull into the water and **λ** is a wavelength parameter related to the panel aspect ratio.

### 2.2.2 Whipping Response
A **whipping vibration** is a transient global hull-girder vibration excited by slamming. The natural frequency **f_w** of whipping is in the range of **5–25 Hz** for typical HSC, overlapping with **local 2-node panel vibrations** — leading to dangerous resonance.

### 2.2.3 Material Selection Rationale
The structural weight of a high speed craft must be **minimized** to maintain payload fraction. Typical materials:

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | Yield Strength (MPa) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Marine Aluminium (5083, 5383, 6082) | 2700 | 215–290 | Patrol boats, fast ferries |
| GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic) | 1500–1900 | 200–400 | Recreational craft, planing boats |
| Carbon Fibre Composite | 1500–1600 | 600–1500 | Racing yachts, high-performance craft |
| High-Tensile Steel (HTS) | 7850 | 355–460 | Large SWATH, military HSC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** The structural weight fraction **W_st / W_total** for a high speed craft must be **≤ 25–30%**, whereas for a tanker it can be 50–60%.

## 2.3 Special Hydrodynamic Features

### 2.3.1 Porpoising
**Porpoising** is a dynamic instability in which the craft undergoes a **self-excited longitudinal oscillation** in heave and pitch. It is *the* classic HSC problem.

**Causes of Porpoising:**
- Centre of gravity too far aft
- Insufficient dynamic lift at the transom
- Excessive trim angle at speed
- Improper interceptor / flap setting

**Stabilizing Condition (Savitsky's criterion, simplified):**
$$\tau_s \geq 2^{\circ} \text{ to } 4^{\circ}$$
where **τ_s** is the static trim angle at design speed.

### 2.3.2 Broaching
A **broach** occurs when a planing craft running with a following sea **surges sideways and broaches to** — a catastrophic loss of directional control. It is most dangerous at **Fn > 0.6** in quartering/following seas.

### 2.3.3 Spray Generation
At planing speeds, water is thrown upwards and sideways from the **chine region**, creating:
- **Sheet spray** (forward, low arc)
- **Rooster tail** (vertical, behind the craft)

This affects **visibility, superstructure loading, and fender design**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Domain / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Froude Number | $F_n = V / \sqrt{g L_{WL}}$ | All HSC, dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = V L_{WL} / \nu$ | Frictional resistance, ν = 1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s for seawater |
| Specific Resistance | $R / \Delta = R / (\rho g \nabla)$ | Used for powering comparisons |
| Power-to-Weight | $P / \Delta$ in kW/tonne | Typical HSC: 1.5–6.0 kW/tonne |
| Block Coefficient (planing) | $C_B = 0.35 - 0.50$ | Displacement: 0.6–0.85 |
| Beam-to-Length Ratio | $B / L$ | Planing: 0.25–0.40 |
| Length-to-Beam Ratio | $L / B$ | Multi-hulls: 5–10 |
| Density Ratio (Al vs Steel) | $\rho_{Al} / \rho_{steel} = 0.34$ | Weight savings ≈ 50–60% |
| Savitsky Planing Trim | $\tau = (\lambda - 0.0065 \cdot B C_\Delta) / (0.012 \cdot \sqrt{\lambda})$ | λ = L_C/B |
| Slamming Pressure | $p_{slam} = 0.5 \rho V_n^2 \pi \lambda$ | Faltinsen (local) |
| HSC Code Speed Criterion | $V_{max} \geq 3.7 \nabla^{0.1667}$ | m/s, with ∇ in m³ |
| Wave Resistance Coef. (Havelock) | $C_{RW} = a \cdot F_n^b$ | Empirical, a, b are constants |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often confuse the **block coefficient** definitions. In high speed craft, **C_B is dramatically lower** (0.35–0.50) than in conventional ships. A high C_B on a planing hull means *inefficient* hull form.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding HSC special features is essential for:
- **Naval Architecture:** Designing fast patrol boats, fast attack craft (FAC), and littoral combat ships.
- **Commercial Shipping:** Fast ferries (Incat, Stena HSS, Fjord Cat).
- **Offshore Industry:** Crew transfer vessels (CTV) for wind farms at speeds >25 knots.
- **Racing Yachts:** America's Cup catamarans and AC75 foiling monohulls.
- **Coast Guard / SAR:** Craft that must reach distressed vessels quickly in rough seas.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The HSC Code Speed Criterion

The IMO HSC Code defines a craft as "high speed" if its **maximum operational speed** exceeds a certain threshold related to displacement. This threshold comes from a **practical envelope of existing fast craft**, not from first principles. The relationship is:

$$V_{max} \geq 3.7 \cdot \nabla^{0.1667} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

**Physical interpretation of the exponent 1/6 ≈ 0.1667:**

The exponent **1/6** comes from the **cube-square law** applied to wave-making resistance. Wave resistance scales roughly as:
$$R_W \propto \nabla^2 \cdot F_n^4$$

Setting **P = R_T × V**, and assuming power available scales as ∇^(2/3) (engine mass proportional to power), one obtains a speed-displacement relation. The constant **3.7** is calibrated against known HSC populations.

**Numerical Example (KTU style):**

A fast ferry has a displacement volume of **∇ = 1000 m³**. Determine whether it is classified as a high speed craft at a service speed of **V = 35 knots**.

**Step 1: Convert knots to m/s**
$$V = 35 \times 0.5144 = 18.00 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the threshold speed**
$$V_{threshold} = 3.7 \times (1000)^{0.1667}$$

$$= 3.7 \times (10^3)^{1/6} = 3.7 \times 10^{0.5} = 3.7 \times 3.1623 = 11.70 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compare**
$$V_{service} = 18.00 \text{ m/s} \; > \; V_{threshold} = 11.70 \text{ m/s} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The vessel **is** a high speed craft under the HSC Code.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Savitsky's Planing Trim Formula

The **Savitsky formula** is the cornerstone of planing hull analysis. For a **hard-chine planing hull** with deadrise angle **β**, beam **B**, and running trim angle **τ**, the mean wet length **l** and wetted length-to-beam ratio **λ = l/B** are related by:

$$\lambda = 0.75 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{C_V}}{\tan \tau} \cdot \left(\frac{1 + (something)??\right)$$

The *canonical* Savitsky formula for **mean wetted length ratio** at given speed is:

$$\lambda = \frac{0.75 \, \sqrt{C_V}}{10 \cdot \sin \tau \cdot \cos \tau} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{1 - \sin \tau}{1 + \sin \tau}\right)^{1.5}\right]^{-1}$$

A more practical **working form** often used in design is:

$$\tau = \frac{0.0065 \cdot B \cdot C_\Delta - \lambda}{0.012 \, \sqrt{\lambda}}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation of the Trim Equation (for a prismatic planing hull):**

**Step 1:** Define the **load coefficient** C_Δ as the ratio of displacement to planing area cubed:
$$C_\Delta = \frac{\Delta}{0.5 \, \rho \, V^2 \, B^2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply vertical force balance: dynamic lift L equals weight W.
$$L = 0.5 \rho V^2 B^2 C_L = \Delta \, g$$

**Step 3:** Apply moment balance about the transom.
- Hydrodynamic pressure moment depends on **trim angle τ** and **wetted length λB**.
- Gravitational moment depends on **LCG position**.

**Step 4:** Combining, the empirical Savitsky formula is:
$$C_{L\lambda} = \tau^{0.1} \cdot \left[ \lambda + 0.0065 \cdot \frac{B C_\Delta}{\tau} \right]$$

**Step 5:** Iterating with **C_Lλ = C_Δ / τ²** (from lift balance) gives the practical trim equation used in design offices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** A typical planing craft at design speed operates at **τ = 3° to 5°**. If τ exceeds ~6°, porpoising risk rises sharply. Designers add **stern flaps or interceptors** to keep τ in the stable band.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slamming Pressure – Faltinsen's Approach

When a flat panel enters water with normal velocity **V_n**, a hydrodynamic pressure pulse develops.

**Step 1: Velocity potential for a 2D wedge impact**
Using the **Wagner** solution:
$$\phi(x, z, t) = -V_n \cdot \text{Re} \sqrt{(c - z)(c + z)}$$

**Step 2: Apply Bernoulli**
$$p = -\rho \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3: Total force on the panel**
$$F = 0.5 \pi \rho V_n^2 \, b^2$$
where **b** is the half-length of the wet footprint.

**Step 4: Convert to pressure**
For a panel of breadth **B_p** and length **l_p**:
$$p_{slam} = 0.5 \rho V_n^2 \pi \lambda$$

where **λ** is the **aspect ratio factor**:
$$\lambda = 1.5 \cdot \frac{l_p^2}{B_p^{1.5}}$$

**Step 5: Numerical example**
- Craft speed: V = 30 knots = 15.43 m/s
- Bottom panel: l_p = 1.5 m, B_p = 2.0 m
- Slamming: V_n = 0.5 × V = 7.72 m/s (50% of service speed is typical impact normal velocity)

Compute λ:
$$\lambda = 1.5 \cdot \frac{1.5^2}{2.0^{1.5}} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{2.25}{2.828} = 1.193$$

Compute slamming pressure:
$$p_{slam} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 7.72^2 \times \pi \times 1.193$$

$$= 512.5 \times 59.60 \times 3.749 = 114{,}580 \text{ Pa} \approx 114.6 \text{ kPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Always use **ρ = 1025 kg/m³ for seawater** and **1000 kg/m³ for freshwater**. Marks are often lost by using 1000 universally.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Reynolds Number for HSC

**Step 1: Definition**
$$Re = \frac{V \cdot L_{WL}}{\nu}$$

**Step 2: For a 30 m craft at 35 knots**
- V = 35 × 0.5144 = 18.0 m/s
- L_WL = 30 m
- ν = 1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s (seawater at 15°C)

**Step 3: Compute**
$$Re = \frac{18.0 \times 30}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{540}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.54 \times 10^8$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**
This Re corresponds to a **fully turbulent boundary layer** on the wetted hull — no laminar flow correction is needed in resistance estimation.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HSC vs Conventional Ship

| Parameter | Conventional Ship | High Speed Craft |
|---|---|---|
| Typical Speed | 12–25 knots | 30–60+ knots |
| Froude Number (Fn) | 0.15–0.30 | 0.4–1.5+ |
| Block Coefficient (C_B) | 0.60–0.85 | 0.30–0.55 |
| Length-to-Beam Ratio (L/B) | 5–8 | 3–6 (planing), 5–12 (multi-hull) |
| Material (hull) | Mild steel | Aluminium / GRP / Composite |
| Structural Weight Fraction | 40–60% | 20–30% |
| Dominant Resistance | Wave + Frictional | Spray + Frictional + Wave |
| Power-to-Weight | 0.05–0.2 kW/tonne | 1.5–6.0 kW/tonne |
| Propulsion | Fixed pitch prop | Waterjet / Surface-piercing prop |
| Stability Basis | GM (metacentric) | Dynamic (trim, lift) |
| Regulatory Code | SOLAS | **HSC Code** |
| Critical Failure Mode | Flooding | **Slamming / Porpoising** |
| Time in Dry-dock | Years | <1 year (high maintenance cycle) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
> *Define a High Speed Craft as per the IMO HSC Code. State the speed-displacement threshold formula and explain its physical basis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **High Speed Craft (HSC)** is defined under the IMO **International Code of Safety for High Speed Craft (1994, amended 2000)** as a craft whose **maximum speed in m/s is at least**:

$$V_{max} \geq 3.7 \nabla^{0.1667}$$

where **∇** is the volume of displacement in **m³**.

**Physical basis:** The exponent **1/6 (≈ 0.1667)** arises from the **cube-square law** applied to wave-making resistance. As displacement scales with **L³** and power scales with **L²**, the relationship between speed and displacement at the boundary of high-speed operation follows this 1/6 power. The constant **3.7** is empirically calibrated against the population of existing high-speed vessels worldwide.

*Example:* A 1000 m³ craft must exceed **11.7 m/s (≈ 22.7 knots)** to qualify as HSC. *(Marks: Definition – 1, Formula – 1, Physical basis – 1)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
> *List any six special features that distinguish a high speed craft from a conventional displacement ship.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **High Froude Number (Fn > 0.4)** — planing or foil-borne regime
2. **Slamming and whipping loads** — bottom panel design critical
3. **Dynamic instabilities** — porpoising, broaching
4. **Lightweight materials** — aluminium, GRP, composites
5. **High power-to-displacement ratio** — 1.5–6.0 kW/tonne
6. **Non-conventional propulsors** — waterjets, surface-piercing propellers

*(Marks: Any six — ½ each)*
