# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "Block Coefficient" of a monohull. What is its typical range for a high-speed planing monohull? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Block Coefficient (C_B)** is the ratio of the displaced volume of the hull to the volume of a rectangular box of length L_WL, beam B, and draft T enclosing it.

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Typical Range:** For high-speed planing monohulls, C_B is **0.35 – 0.45** (slender, low-volume forms), whereas for full-displacement cargo monohulls it is 0.65 – 0.80. A lower C_B reduces wave-making resistance but reduces cargo capacity.

**Marks Distribution (per KTU pattern):**
- Definition with formula: **2 marks**
- Typical value for planing: **1 mark**

---

### Q2. State the Froude number based on waterline length. Why is it important for monohull design? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$$

This dimensionless ratio compares the vessel's forward speed (V) to the celerity of a gravity wave of length equal to the waterline length. **Importance:**
1. It governs **wave-making resistance** (which peaks near F_n ≈ 0.25 for conventional ships, the "hump").
2. It determines whether the hull operates in **displacement, semi-displacement, or planing** regime.
3. It is the primary scaling parameter used in model tests and extrapolation to full scale (Froude scaling).

**Marks Distribution:**
- Formula: **1 mark**
- Significance (any 2 of the 3 above): **2 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A — Full Module Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`

**A monohull planing craft has the following particulars:**
- Length overall L_OA = 18 m, Waterline length L_WL = 16 m
- Beam B = 4 m, Draft T = 0.9 m (mean)
- Displacement ∇ = 36 m³ (seawater, ρ = 1025 kg/m³)
- Wetted surface area S = 78 m²
- Design speed V = 22 knots = 11.31 m/s
- Kinematic viscosity ν = 1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s

**Calculate:**

**(a)** (7 marks) The displacement (in tonnes), block coefficient, volume Froude number, and identify the operating regime.

**(b)** (7 marks) The frictional resistance (in kN) using the ITTC-1978 line with form factor k = 0.15, and the corresponding effective power (in kW).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Displacement in tonnes:**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1025 \times 36 = 36{,}900 \text{ kg} = 36.9 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — Block coefficient:**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{36}{16 \times 4 \times 0.9} = \frac{36}{57.6} = 0.625$$

**Step 3 — Volume Froude number:**

$$\nabla^{1/3} = 36^{1/3} = 3.302 \text{ m}$$

$$F_{n\nabla} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot \nabla^{1/3}}} = \frac{11.31}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 3.302}} = \frac{11.31}{\sqrt{32.39}} = \frac{11.31}{5.691} \approx 1.987$$

**Step 4 — Regime Identification:**

Since F_n∇ ≈ 1.99 > 1.5, the craft is in the **PLANING REGIME**.

**Result (a):** Δ = 36.9 t, C_B = 0.625, F_n∇ = 1.99 → Planing regime. **Full marks = 7.**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Reynolds number:**

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{WL}}{\nu} = \frac{11.31 \times 16}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{180.96}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.521 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 — ITTC frictional coefficient:**

$$\log_{10} R_n = \log_{10}(1.521 \times 10^{8}) = 8.182$$

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(8.182 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.182)^2} = \frac{0.075}{38.22} = 1.962 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Apply form factor k = 0.15:**

$$C_F^{hull} = k \cdot C_F = 0.15 \times 1.962 \times 10^{-3} = 2.943 \times 10^{-4}$$

(Alternative convention: multiply final R_F by (1+k); state your assumption explicitly.)

**Step 4 — Frictional resistance:**

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F^{hull}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (11.31)^2 \times 78 \times 2.943 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 127.92 \times 78 \times 2.943 \times 10^{-4}$$

Computing stepwise:
- 0.5 × 1025 = 512.5
- 512.5 × 127.92 = 65,559
- 65,559 × 78 = 5,113,602
- 5,113,602 × 2.943 × 10⁻⁴ ≈ 1505 N

$$R_F \approx 1.505 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Effective power:**

$$P_E = R_F \cdot V = 1505 \times 11.31 = 17{,}021 \text{ W} \approx 17.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Result (b):** R_F ≈ 1.51 kN, P_E ≈ 17.0 kW (frictional component only; total P_E would include wave + spray). **Full marks = 7.**

---

### QUESTION B — Full Module Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**A semi-displacement monohull has the following particulars:**
- L_WL = 28 m, B = 6 m, T = 1.6 m
- Midship coefficient C_M = 0.78
- Prismatic coefficient C_P = 0.62
- Hull form factor k = 0.18

**(a)** (7 marks) Calculate the block coefficient, displacement (in m³ and tonnes), and verify the identity C_B = C_P · C_M.

**(b)** (7 marks) At V = 12 knots (= 6.17 m/s), determine the Froude number, Reynolds number, ITTC frictional coefficient, and effective frictional resistance (assume S = 220 m², ν = 1.19 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — Block coefficient from identity:**

$$C_B = C_P \cdot C_M = 0.62 \times 0.78 = 0.4836 \approx 0.484$$

**Step 2 — Verification using direct formula:**

$$\nabla = L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B = 28 \times 6 \times 1.6 \times 0.484 = 28 \times 9.6 \times 0.484 = 268.8 \times 0.484 = 130.10 \text{ m}^3$$

Verification via C_P:
$$A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T = 0.78 \times 6 \times 1.6 = 7.488 \text{ m}^2$$
$$\nabla_{via \, C_P} = C_P \cdot A_M \cdot L_{WL} = 0.62 \times 7.488 \times 28 = 130.10 \text{ m}^3 \;\; ✓$$

**Step 3 — Displacement mass:**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1025 \times 130.10 = 133{,}353 \text{ kg} \approx 133.4 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Result (a):** C_B = 0.484, ∇ = 130.1 m³, Δ = 133.4 t, identity verified. **Full marks = 7.**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Froude number:**

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}} = \frac{6.17}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 28}} = \frac{6.17}{\sqrt{274.68}} = \frac{6.17}{16.575} \approx 0.372$$

Since F_n < 0.4, the craft is in **displacement regime**.

**Step 2 — Reynolds number:**

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{WL}}{\nu} = \frac{6.17 \times 28}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{172.76}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.452 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3 — ITTC frictional coefficient:**

$$\log_{10}(1.452 \times 10^{8}) = 8.162$$

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(8.162 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(6.162)^2} = \frac{0.075}{37.97} \approx 1.975 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Frictional resistance with k = 0.18:**

$$C_F^{hull} = 0.18 \times 1.975 \times 10^{-3} = 3.555 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F^{hull}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 38.07 \times 220 \times 3.555 \times 10^{-4}$$

Computing stepwise:
- 0.5 × 1025 = 512.5
- 512.5 × 38.07 = 19,510.9
- 19,510.9 × 220 = 4,292,398
- 4,292,398 × 3.555 × 10⁻⁴ ≈ 1526 N

$$R_F \approx 1.526 \text{ kN}$$

**Result (b):** F_n = 0.372 (displacement), R_n = 1.45 × 10⁸, C_F = 1.975 × 10⁻³, R_F ≈ 1.53 kN. **Full marks = 7.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Monohull Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units / sign conventions (–1 to –2 marks):** Always state the unit (m, m², m³, kg, tonnes, kN, kW) and write down intermediate values to the calculator before applying the next operation.
> 2. **Mixing up C_B, C_P, C_M (–2 marks):** When asked "compute C_B given C_P and C_M," students often write C_B = C_M / C_P — this is **wrong**. The correct identity is **C_B = C_P × C_M**.
> 3. **Wrong speed conversion (–2 marks):** 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s**, not 0.5 m/s. A craft at 22 knots is 11.32 m/s, not 11.0 m/s. Always show the conversion factor.
> 4. **ITTC formula transcribed incorrectly (–2 marks):** The denominator is **(log₁₀ R_n − 2)²**, not (log R_n − 2.5)² or (log R_n)². One wrong number ruins the result.
> 5. **Form factor misuse (–2 marks):** Some texts apply k multiplicatively to C_F (giving C_F_hull = k·C_F), others use (1+k). State the convention **explicitly** at the top of your answer.
> 6. **Forgetting to verify identity (–1 mark):** When both C_B and (C_P · C_M) are asked, end with a boxed "Verified" or "✓" statement.
> 7. **Skipping regime identification (–1 to –2 marks):** When F_n∇ is computed, always close with a one-line conclusion: "Since F_n∇ > 1.5, the craft is in the PLANING REGIME."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Monohull Module 1 — High-Density Recall Sheet**
>
> ✅ A monohull is a **single-hulled craft** operating in displacement, semi-displacement, or planing regimes.
>
> ✅ Governing dimensionless numbers: **F_n (wave), R_n (viscous), F_n∇ (regime)**.
>
> ✅ **Core identity to memorize verbatim:** C_B = C_P × C_M.
>
> ✅ **Form coefficients** describe hull fullness in 3-D: C_B (overall), C_P (longitudinal), C_M (transverse), C_WP (waterplane).
>
> ✅ **Planing threshold:** F_n∇ > 1.5.
>
> ✅ **ITTC-78 line:** C_F = 0.075 / (log R_n − 2)². Form factor k ≈ 0.10 – 0.20.
>
> ✅ **Savitsky planing R_T:** R_TB = Δ / tan τ for V ≥ V_min; apply sec β correction for deadrise.
>
> ✅ **Speed conversion to memorize:** 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s**; 1 m/s = **1.944 knots**.
>
> ✅ **Standard densities:** ρ_seawater = **1025 kg/m³**, ρ_freshwater = **1000 kg/m³**.
>
> ✅ **Wetted surface area (WSA)** is a derived geometric quantity; use Series 62 or similar empirical formulas.
>
> ✅ **Effective power:** P_E = R_T × V (in W when R_T in N and V in m/s).
>
> ✅ **LCB vs. LCG:** LCB controls *natural* trim at rest; LCG controls *static and dynamic* trim under load. Keep LCG slightly aft of LCB for acceptable bow-down attitude.
>
> ✅ **Transom design** matters in planing: too low → slam; too high → resistance penalty.
>
> ✅ **Deadrise angle** (β) increases slamming resistance reduction but raises planing R_T via sec β.
>
> ✅ **Hull slenderness ratio L/∇^(1/3)** > 6 → slender hull, wave resistance dominant; < 5 → bluff, frictional dominant.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning Craft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST745 - DESIGN OF HIGH SPEED CRAFTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Planning Craft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planning Craft (Preliminary Design Phase)** is the systematic, iterative conceptual and analytical process undertaken *before* detailed engineering drawings are produced, in which the high-speed craft's **mission requirements, operational envelope, principal dimensions, hull form family, displacement, weight, powering, and cost envelope** are translated from a *set of operational needs* into a *coherent, feasible, and economically justified design baseline*.

In the strict KTU / NEP 2020 parlance, planning (also called *preliminary* or *contract* design) is the **first of three classical naval-architecture design phases**:

| Phase | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Planning (Concept)** | Feasibility, sizing, selection | General Arrangement, Baseline Specs |
| **Contract** | Lines plan, weights, powering, stability | Lines drawing, Curves of form |
| **Detailed** | Structural scantlings, systems integration | Production drawings, Class approval |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House Blueprint"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Before a builder pours concrete, an architect draws a **blueprint** specifying *how many rooms, what size, where the load-bearing walls go, and what the budget looks like*. The owner (mission customer) says *"I want a coastal home for a family of four, hurricane-resistant, budget ₹1.5 Cr."*
>
> In ship design, the **owner says**: *"I want a 35 m patrol boat, 30 knots, 250 nm range, monsoon-compliant, budget $4 M."* The naval architect's job in the **planning phase** is to translate that statement into a **main dimensions sketch, an estimated weight, an estimated power, and a hull-form family** — *before* a single plate is cut.

The planning phase is therefore a **decision-making funnel** — every choice made here constrains all 200+ downstream design decisions.

## 1.3 Why Planning is the *Most Critical* Phase

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students treat planning as *"just rough calculations."* In reality, *the planning phase commits roughly 75–80 % of the lifecycle cost and locks in ~90 % of the performance parameters.* A wrong assumption in planning is **irreversible** once the contract is signed.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Craft Planning

Every high-speed craft plan must simultaneously balance three competing pillars (often called the **"Design Triangle"**):

1. **Performance** — speed, acceleration, range, seakeeping
2. **Cost** — build cost, operating cost, life-cycle cost
3. **Compliance** — Classification society rules, regulatory body rules (MCA, IMO, IRS), mission-specific regulations

```
                PERFORMANCE
                     /\
                    /  \
                   /    \
                  / PLAN \
                 /        \
                /__________\
            COMPLIANCE ---- COST
```

Mathematically, the planning process can be viewed as a constrained multi-objective optimisation:

$$\min_{\vec{x}} \; \left[ C(\vec{x}),\; \frac{1}{P(\vec{x})},\; R(\vec{x}) \right] \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \vec{g}(\vec{x}) \le 0, \; \vec{x} \in \mathcal{F}$$

where $\vec{x} = [L, B, T, \Delta, C_B, V, \dots]$ is the design vector, $C$ is cost, $P$ is performance, $R$ is risk (regulatory), $\vec{g}$ are the constraint functions, and $\mathcal{F}$ is the feasibility region.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Spiral — Iterative Refinement

The **Design Spiral** (originally proposed by John Andrews, 1954, and still a KTU-favoured conceptual model) formalises planning as a series of nested, **converging loops**. Each pass adds a layer of detail; the spiral tightens as the design matures.

**Why a spiral and not a straight line?**
Because decisions are **coupled** — you cannot pick $L$ without knowing $\Delta$, and you cannot pick $\Delta$ without knowing $L$. Only iteration converges.

### Step-by-step Logic of the Design Spiral

1. **Define mission** — what the craft *must* do (mission profile).
2. **Set operational requirements** — top speed, range, payload, sea state.
3. **Choose hull form family** — planing, semi-displacement, displacement, SWATH, catamaran, SES, ACV.
4. **Estimate main dimensions** — $L_{OA}$, $L_{WL}$, $B$, $T$, $D$.
5. **Estimate displacement** — lightship + deadweight.
6. **Estimate resistance & power** — Froude scaling, statistical methods.
7. **Check feasibility** — stability, seakeeping, structural weight.
8. **Iterate / Revise** — go back to step 3 or 4 if any constraint violated.
9. **Cost check & regulatory check** — verify within budget and rules.
10. **Lock baseline** — freeze parameters for contract design.

## 2.2 Hull-Form Family Selection — Decision Matrix

The single most consequential planning decision. KTU 2024 expects students to **justify** the family choice, not just state it.

| Hull Form | Best Speed Regime | Slenderness $L/\nabla^{1/3}$ | Typical Speed-Length $V/\sqrt{L}$ | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Displacement** (round-bilge) | Sub-critical | $5.0 - 6.5$ | $0.4 - 0.9$ | Tugs, trawlers |
| **Semi-displacement** | Trans-critical | $6.0 - 7.5$ | $0.9 - 1.5$ | Patrol boats, pilot |
| **Planing** (V-bottom) | Super-critical | n/a (dynamic lift) | $1.5 - 3.0$ | Rescue, racing |
| **Catamaran** | $1.0 - 2.5$ | $8.0 - 12.0$ | $1.0 - 2.5$ | Ferries, fast crew |
| **SWATH** | $1.0 - 1.8$ | $8.0 - 10.0$ | $1.0 - 1.8$ | Stable research |
| **SES** (Surface Effect Ship) | $1.5 - 2.5$ | $8.0 - 11.0$ | $1.5 - 2.5$ | Military fast attack |
| **ACV** (Air-Cushion) | $1.0 - 1.8$ | n/a | $1.0 - 1.8$ | Amphibious |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Savitsky / Blount):** Once $V/\sqrt{L_{WL}} > 1.34$, a displacement hull starts *planing* and resistance grows non-linearly. This is the **planing threshold** — the key discriminator for high-speed craft.

## 2.3 Speed-Length Ratio — The Master Discriminator

$$\frac{V}{\sqrt{L_{WL}}} = F_n \sqrt{g}$$

A dimensionless proxy used in KTU board questions:

$$SLR \;=\; \frac{V \text{ [knots]}}{\sqrt{L_{WL} \text{ [ft]}}}$$

- $SLR < 0.9$ → **displacement regime**
- $0.9 \le SLR \le 1.5$ → **semi-displacement**
- $SLR > 1.5$ → **planing regime**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Planning Phase (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this table.** KTU Part B 14-mark questions on planning often expect direct substitution into one of these.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g L_{WL}}}$ | Froude number | — | All regimes |
| 2 | $V_{kn} = 0.5144 \; V_{m/s}$ | Speed conversion | kn / m·s⁻¹ | All |
| 3 | $\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho}$ | Displacement volume | m³ | All |
| 4 | $\Delta = \rho g \nabla = W_{total}$ | Weight = buoyancy | t, kN | All |
| 5 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{WL} B T}$ | Block coefficient | — | $0.35 - 0.85$ |
| 6 | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M L_{WL}}$ | Prismatic coefficient | — | $0.55 - 0.85$ |
| 7 | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B T}$ | Midship coeff | — | $0.70 - 0.98$ |
| 8 | $C_W = \dfrac{A_W}{L_{WL} B}$ | Waterplane coeff | — | $0.65 - 0.90$ |
| 9 | $SLR = V_{kn}/\sqrt{L_{ft}}$ | Speed-length ratio | kn/√ft | All |
| 10 | $C_A = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} V^3}{EHP}$ | Admiralty coeff | — | Statistical |
| 11 | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ (CV) | Effective horsepower | hp | Resistance |
| 12 | $SHP = EHP / \eta_D / \eta_S$ | Shaft horsepower | hp | Powering |
| 13 | $L_{OA} = L_{WL}(1 + CB\%)$ | Length overall | m | Dimensioning |
| 14 | $W_{LS} = C_{LS} \cdot L^{a} B^{b} D^{c}$ | Lightship weight | t | Statistical |
| 15 | $B/T = f(F_n, C_B)$ | Beam-draft ratio | — | Stability |

> **Constants to memorise (bold):**
> - Seawater density $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1.025 \; t/m^3}$ (or $\mathbf{1025 \; kg/m^3}$)
> - Gravity $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
> - 1 knot = $\mathbf{0.5144 \; m/s}$

## 2.5 Displacement Estimation (Weight Budget)

The weight budget is **additive**:
$$\Delta_{total} = W_{LS} + W_{DW}$$

where $W_{LS}$ = lightship (hull, machinery, outfit) and $W_{DW}$ = deadweight (payload, fuel, crew, stores, water).

### Statistical Lightship Weight Formula (Watson-Gilfillan / AEGL)

$$W_{LS} = C_{LS} \cdot L^{a} B^{b} D^{c}$$

Typical exponents for high-speed craft (KDS / DNV SSV data):
- $L$: exponent $1.3 - 1.7$
- $B$: exponent $0.5 - 0.8$
- $D$: exponent $0.4 - 0.6$

## 2.6 Powering Estimation — Admiralty Coefficient Method

When the planning engineer has *no* model-test data, the **Admiralty Coefficient** is the quickest estimate:

$$C_A = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{EHP}$$

For **high-speed patrol craft**, typical $C_A$ values:
- $C_A \approx 250 - 400$ (semi-displacement, modern form)
- $C_A \approx 180 - 280$ (planing hulls, lower efficiency at high speed)

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher $C_A$ = more efficient hull at the given speed-displacement combination.**

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Practice

| Sector | Why Planning Drives Everything |
|---|---|
| **Defence (Naval)** | RFP (Request For Proposal) compliance is set in planning |
| **Commercial Ferries** | Route economics depend on Bunker/day = $f$(EHP, $\Delta$) |
| **SAR / Coast Guard** | Response time $\propto 1/V$ — direct mission metric |
| **Offshore OSV** | Fuel cap = 30 % OPEX; sensitivity to $C_B$ is critical |
| **Recreational** | Hull choice drives handling feel — owner-specific |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Every 14-mark planning problem is solved by **deriving the relationships first, then substituting the owner's numbers.** Below are the canonical derivations a 2024-scheme student must master.

## 3.1 Derivation — Froude Number from Dimensional Analysis

**Premise:** Resistance $R$ depends on fluid density $\rho$, velocity $V$, characteristic length $L$, gravity $g$, and viscosity $\mu$.

$$R = f(\rho, V, L, g, \mu)$$

**Total variables:** 6 ; **Fundamental dimensions** (M, L, T): 3 ; so **Buckingham Pi groups** = $6 - 3 = 3$.

Choose repeating variables: $\rho, V, L$.

**Pi 1** — Force-like:
$$\Pi_1 = \frac{R}{\rho V^2 L^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_T = \frac{R}{0.5 \rho V^2 S}$$

**Pi 2** — Froude (inertia-gravity ratio):
$$\Pi_2 = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} = F_n$$

**Pi 3** — Reynolds:
$$\Pi_3 = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = R_n$$

Hence:
$$C_T = f(F_n, R_n, \text{form})$$

For free-surface, fully-turbulent flows, $R_n$ effect collapses and:
$$C_T \approx f(F_n, \text{form})$$

**This is the ITTC 1978 extrapolation basis.**

## 3.2 Derivation — Block Coefficient from Volume Definition

By geometric definition:
$$C_B = \frac{\text{Actual displaced volume}}{\text{Box (prism) volume}}$$

$$\nabla_{box} = L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T$$

$$\nabla_{actual} = \int_0^{L_{WL}} A(x) \, dx$$

Therefore:
$$C_B = \frac{\nabla_{actual}}{L_{WL} B T}$$

Splitting by the prism theorem $\nabla = A_M \cdot L_{WL} \cdot C_P$ and $A_M = B \cdot T \cdot C_M$:

$$C_B = \frac{A_M \cdot L_{WL} \cdot C_P}{B \cdot T \cdot L_{WL}} = C_P \cdot C_M$$

$$\boxed{\;C_B = C_P \cdot C_M\;}$$

**Practical KTU takeaway:** In high-speed craft, $C_M$ is fixed by structural scantlings ($C_M \approx 0.85 - 0.95$). $C_B$ drops with speed because $C_P$ drops (finer ends).

## 3.3 Derivation — Speed-Length Ratio (SLR)

Starting from Froude number:
$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}}$$

Substitute $V$ in knots and $L$ in feet:
$$V_{kn} = 0.5144 \, V_{m/s}, \quad L_{ft} = 0.3048 \, L_m$$

Compute $\sqrt{g L_{ft}}$:
$$\sqrt{g L_{ft}} = \sqrt{32.2 \cdot L_{ft}} \;\; [\text{ft/s}]$$

Convert to knots: divide by $0.5144$
$$\sqrt{g L_{ft}} \; [kn] = \frac{\sqrt{32.2 \, L_{ft}}}{0.5144} = 1.34 \sqrt{L_{ft}}$$

Therefore:
$$F_n = \frac{V_{kn}}{1.34 \sqrt{L_{ft}}}$$

Define:
$$\boxed{\;SLR = \frac{V_{kn}}{\sqrt{L_{ft}}} = 1.34 \cdot F_n\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Past-Year Pattern:** A common 7-mark sub-question is: *"If $V = 25$ kn and $L_{WL} = 100$ ft, classify the hull regime."* Answer: $SLR = 25/10 = 2.5$ → **planing regime**.

## 3.4 Derivation — Admiralty Coefficient (Statistical EHP)

Assume a similarity law where $EHP \propto \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3$ (from Froude scaling for wave-making resistance, dominant in high-speed craft):
$$EHP = K \cdot \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3$$

Group the constant $K$ as the *Admiralty Coefficient* $C_A$:
$$\boxed{\;C_A = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{EHP}\;}$$

Solving for EHP:
$$EHP = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{C_A}$$

**Units check:** $\Delta$ in tonnes (or long tons), $V$ in knots, $EHP$ in hp, $C_A$ is dimensionless.

## 3.5 Derivation — Total Resistance Decomposition (ITTC Standard)

$$R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A + R_{APP}$$

Where:
- $R_F$ = frictional (ITTC-57 correlation)
- $R_R$ = residuary (wave + eddy, from model test)
- $R_A$ = correlation allowance (typically $0.0004 \cdot \Delta$)
- $R_{APP}$ = appendage resistance

**ITTC-57 frictional line:**
$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2}$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F$$

**Effective Power:**
$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} \quad \text{(CV, metric)}$$

or
$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{550} \quad \text{(hp, imperial)}$$

**Shaft Power:**
$$SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S \cdot \eta_G}$$

For high-speed craft, total propulsive efficiency $\eta_D = \eta_H \eta_R \eta_S \eta_G \approx 0.55 - 0.65$.

## 3.6 Derivation — Length Overall from Waterline Length

Allowance for stem overhang and transom overhang:
$$L_{OA} = L_{WL} + L_{stem} + L_{transom}$$

Empirically, for modern high-speed craft:
$$L_{OA} \approx 1.05 \cdot L_{WL} \quad \text{(typical)}$$

$$\boxed{\;L_{OA} = L_{WL} \cdot (1 + 0.05)\;}$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Typical Parametric Bands for High-Speed Craft

| Parameter | Symbol | Planing | Semi-Disp. | Catamaran | SES |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Length/Beam | $L/B$ | $3.0 - 4.5$ | $4.5 - 6.0$ | $8.0 - 12.0$ | $8.0 - 11.0$ |
| Beam/Draft | $B/T$ | $2.5 - 4.0$ | $2.0 - 3.0$ | $2.5 - 4.0$ | $3.0 - 5.0$ |
| Block Coeff | $C_B$ | $0.35 - 0.45$ | $0.45 - 0.60$ | $0.40 - 0.55$ | $0.45 - 0.60$ |
| Prismatic | $C_P$ | $0.55 - 0.65$ | $0.60 - 0.70$ | $0.55 - 0.65$ | $0.60 - 0.70$ |
| Midship | $C_M$ | $0.85 - 0.95$ | $0.80 - 0.90$ | $0.85 - 0.95$ | $0.85 - 0.95$ |
| Length/Disp$^{1/3}$ | $L/\nabla^{1/3}$ | n/a | $6.0 - 7.5$ | $8.0 - 12.0$ | $8.0 - 11.0$ |
| SLR | $V_{kn}/\sqrt{L_{ft}}$ | $1.5 - 3.0$ | $0.9 - 1.5$ | $1.0 - 2.5$ | $1.5 - 2.5$ |

## 3.8 Worked Example — Preliminary Sizing of a Patrol Craft

> **Owner requirement:** $V = 28$ kn, $R = 350$ nm, 12 crew + 4 ton payload.

**Step 1 — Choose hull form:** For $V$ in this range and moderate range, **semi-displacement monohull with hard chine** is selected (justifies choice).

**Step 2 — Set slenderness:** $L/\nabla^{1/3} = 6.5$ (mid-range for semi-disp.)

**Step 3 — Estimate displacement from fuel + payload + crew:**
$$W_{DW} = 4 + (12 \times 0.1) + W_{fuel} \approx 5.2 + W_{fuel}$$

Fuel by simple range formula:
$$W_{fuel} = \frac{SFC \cdot SHP \cdot R}{V \cdot \eta_F} \quad [\text{t}]$$

Assume $SFC = 0.22 \; \text{kg/hp·h}$, $\eta_F = 0.95$, tentative $SHP = 1500$ hp:
$$W_{fuel} = \frac{0.22 \times 1500 \times 350}{28 \times 0.95 \times 0.5144 \times 350} \approx 13.3 \text{ t}$$

Then $W_{DW} \approx 18.5$ t. Estimate lightship as 60 % of total:
$$\Delta \approx 30 \; t \Rightarrow \nabla \approx 29.3 \; m^3$$

**Step 4 — Solve for L:**
$$L = 6.5 \times \nabla^{1/3} = 6.5 \times 29.3^{1/3} = 6.5 \times 3.09 \approx 20.1 \; m$$

**Step 5 — Set B and T from $L/B$ and $B/T$ bands:**
$L/B = 5.0 \Rightarrow B = 4.0 \; m$
$B/T = 2.5 \Rightarrow T = 1.6 \; m$

**Step 6 — Check $C_B$:**
$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{LBT} = \frac{29.3}{20.1 \times 4.0 \times 1.6} = 0.228$$

This is **too low** — indicates hull is too fine or $L$ is overestimated. **Iterate**: increase $\Delta$ estimate by adding margin → $L$ reduces → feasible.

**Step 7 — Verify SLR:**
$$SLR = \frac{28}{\sqrt{65.9}} \approx 3.45 \quad \text{(ft, knots)}$$

Wait — convert $L$ to ft: $20.1 \times 3.28 = 65.9$ ft. $SLR = 28/\sqrt{65.9} = 3.45$ → **planing regime** (high). So hull form should switch to planing. **Iterate to step 1.**

> [!WARNING]
> **This is exactly why planning is iterative.** The first pass rarely converges.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Design Spiral — Planning Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stepA_mission] --> B[stepB_operational]
    B --> C[stepC_hullform]
    C --> D[stepD_dimensions]
    D --> E[stepE_displacement]
    E --> F[stepF_powering]
    F --> G[stepG_resistance]
    G --> H{stepH_feasible}
    H -->|NO| C
    H -->|YES| I[stepI_stability]
    I --> J[stepJ_seakeeping]
    J --> K{stepK_compliance}
    K -->|FAIL| D
    K -->|PASS| L[stepL_cost]
    L --> M{stepM_budget}
    M -->|NO| C
    M -->|YES| N[stepN_baseline]
    style N fill:#90EE90
    style A fill:#87CEEB
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each box is a decision node in the planning spiral. Arrows that loop *back* (e.g., `H → C`) are the **iterative revisions** that characterise the spiral. The green node `stepN_baseline` is the **frozen configuration** released to contract design.

## 4.2 Hull-Form Family Selection — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[start_speed_req] --> B{V_kn_over_sqrt_LWL}
    B -->|less than 0.9| C1[displacement_hull]
    B -->|0.9 to 1.5| D1{semi_disp_type}
    B -->|greater than 1.5| E1{mission_range}
    D1 -->|low_range| D2[mono_hull]
    D1 -->|high_range| D3[catamaran]
    E1 -->|under 200 nm| E2[planing_vee]
    E1 -->|200 to 500 nm| E3[SES]
    E1 -->|over 500 nm| E4[high_speed_catamaran]
    C1 --> F[end_choice]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    style F fill:#FFD700
```

> The gold node `end_choice` is the **selected hull form family** — the single most important planning output.

## 4.3 Planning Phase — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[OWNER_INPUTS] --> B1[DESIGN_BASELINE]
    A2[CLASS_RULES] --> B1
    A3[OPERATIONAL_PROFILE] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[WEIGHT_ESTIMATION]
    B1 --> C2[POWERING_ESTIMATION]
    B1 --> C3[STABILITY_PRELIM]
    B1 --> C4[COST_ESTIMATION]
    C1 --> D1[DISPLACEMENT_BASELINE]
    C2 --> D2[MACHINERY_BASELINE]
    C3 --> D3[FORM_BASELINE]
    C4 --> D4[BUDGET_BASELINE]
    D1 --> E1[GA_DRAWING]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[CONTRACT_PHASE_RELEASE]
    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style A2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style A3 fill:#FFB6C1
    style F1 fill:#90EE90
```

> **Reading this block diagram:** Pink nodes are *inputs* (from owner, class, operations). Blue node is the integrated *design baseline*. Four parallel estimation streams (yellow) feed into the four domain baselines (orange) which converge into the *General Arrangement drawing* (light blue) — the tangible output of the planning phase.

## 4.4 Speed-Regime Map (Speed-Length Ratio Discriminator)

```
SPEED-LENGTH RATIO   HULL REGIME          RESISTANCE BEHAVIOUR
     0.0 ──────────  Displacement         R ∝ V^2 (Froude)
         |
     0.9 ──────────  ────  Hump Region ────  R ∝ V^4 - V^6  ★
         |
     1.34 ─────────  ────  Planing Threshold (Savitsky)
         |
     1.5 ──────────  Semi-Planing         R ∝ V^3 (dynamic)
         |
     2.0+ ──────────  Full Planing         R ∝ V^2 (aerodynamic-like)
```

> ★ The **hump** ($F_n \approx 0.25 - 0.40$, or $SLR \approx 0.9 - 1.5$) is the **region of maximum resistance** for displacement hulls. KTU board questions often ask: *"Why does EHP for a 30 kn boat increase drastically when L is decreased below 25 m?"* — Answer: because the hull enters the hump region.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Planning of a craft" and list any four activities performed during the planning phase.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Planning of a craft is the **preliminary design phase** in which the owner's mission requirements are translated into a feasible set of principal dimensions, hull form, displacement, powering, and cost baseline — *before* detailed engineering begins.

Four activities of the planning phase:
1. **Mission definition** — purpose, route, payload, crew.
2. **Operational requirements** — top speed, cruise speed, range, endurance, sea state.
3. **Hull form selection** — displacement, semi-displacement, planing, catamaran, etc.
4. **Main dimensions estimation** — $L_{WL}$, $B$, $T$, $D$ using statistical/parametric methods.
5. *Optional:* Displacement & weight estimation, preliminary EHP/SHP, cost envelope.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing 4 activities — any 4 of the above.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "Speed-Length Ratio" (SLR)? State the SLR value that marks the boundary between displacement and semi-displacement regimes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **SLR definition (1 mark):** Speed-Length Ratio is the dimensionless discriminator given by
$$SLR = \frac{V \; [knots]}{\sqrt{L_{WL} \; [feet]}}$$
It is numerically equal to $1.34 \times F_n$ and indicates the speed regime in which the hull operates.
- **Boundary value (2 marks):**
  - $SLR < 0.9$ → displacement regime
  - $0.9 \le SLR \le 1.5$ → semi-displacement
  - $SLR > 1.5$ → planing regime
  - The **boundary between displacement and semi-displacement** is at $SLR \approx 0.9$ (corresponding to $F_n \approx 0.4$).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Planning Phase:**
> 1. **Don't skip units** — every numeric answer must carry a unit (m, kn, t, hp). Loss: 0.5–1 mark per sub-question.
> 2. **Don't forget justification** — if asked to *choose* a hull form, you *must* justify with numbers (SLR, slenderness, range). A choice without justification gets only 2/7 marks on that part.
> 3. **Iterate if necessary** — the first pass of main dimensions rarely converges; a one-pass solution with $C_B$ out of range loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Always show Admiralty-Coefficient assumption** — examiners check $C_A$ value vs. hull form; using $C_A = 150$ for a planing hull is a giveaway that the student memorised blindly.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

*(a)* **(7 marks)** A high-speed patrol craft is required to operate at **$V = 32$ knots** with a **range of 400 nautical miles**. The craft must carry **14 crew, 6 tonnes of payload, and operate in sea state 4.** Using the planning procedure, select a suitable **hull form family** and justify your choice. State the design constraints and boundary conditions you would impose.

*(b)* **(7 marks)** For the craft in part (a), estimate the **main dimensions** $L_{WL}$, $B$, $T$, and the **preliminary displacement $\Delta$**, given the following data:

- Lightship weight coefficient (Watson-Gilfillan): $W_{LS} = 0.18 L^{1.4} B^{0.7} D^{0.5}$ (with $L$ in m, output in t)
- $C_B = 0.48$ (assumed for chosen hull)
- $B/T = 2.8$, $L/B = 5.5$
- $D = 1.10 \, T$ (depth-draft ratio)

Show all steps and iterate if the first pass is infeasible.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Hull Form Selection (7 marks)**

*Step 1 — Assume initial $L_{WL}$ for SLR calculation:* For a 32 kn patrol boat, $L_{WL}$ is typically 20–28 m. Take $L_{WL} = 24$ m $\Rightarrow L_{WL,ft} = 78.7$ ft.

*Step 2 — Compute SLR:*
$$SLR = \frac{V_{kn}}{\sqrt{L_{ft}}} = \frac{32}{\sqrt{78.7}} = \frac{32}{8.87} = 3.61$$

*Step 3 — Interpret:* $SLR = 3.61 > 1.5$ → **planing regime**.

*Step 4 — Check range:* 400 nm is moderate. Pure planing hulls have limited range (fuel-hungry at high speed). A **semi-displacement hard-chine hull** is more efficient for this range.

*Step 5 — Revised choice:* Adjust $L_{WL}$ upward. Try $L_{WL} = 30$ m $\Rightarrow 98.4$ ft.
$$SLR = \frac{32}{\sqrt{98.4}} = \frac{32}{9.92} = 3.23 \;\;\text{(still planing)}$$

*Step 6 — Final choice:* **Deep-V semi-displacement hard-chine monohull with interceptors** — balances planing efficiency with moderate range capability. Suitable for sea state 4 with proper hull design.

*Justification (2 marks):* SLR > 1.5 confirms need for dynamic lift; sea state 4 demands high freeboard and good slamming resistance (Deep-V); 400 nm range requires fuel efficiency (semi-disp. is best compromise).

*Design constraints (2 marks):*
- Maximum $B$ for harbour/berth constraint (assume $B \le 5.0$ m)
- Maximum draft for coastal operations: $T \le 2.0$ m
- Crew accommodation: 14 persons × 1.5 m²/person = 21 m² minimum
- Range fuel capacity: ≈ 15 t (estimated)
- Classification: Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) – High Speed Craft rules

**Part (b) — Main Dimensions (7 marks)**

*Step 1 — From L/B = 5.5 and B/T = 2.8:*
$$L = 5.5 \, B, \quad B = 2.8 \, T$$
So $L = 5.5 \times 2.8 \, T = 15.4 \, T$.

*Step 2 — Use $C_B$ to relate volume:*
$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{LBT} = 0.48 \Rightarrow \nabla = 0.48 \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$

*Step 3 — Express everything in T:*
$$L = 15.4 \, T, \quad B = 2.8 \, T$$
$$\nabla = 0.48 \times 15.4 \, T \times 2.8 \, T \times T = 20.68 \, T^3$$

*Step 4 — Estimate weight budget:*
- Payload: 6 t
- Crew + effects: $14 \times 0.1 = 1.4$ t
- Fuel (rough): 12 t (from $W_{fuel} = SFC \cdot SHP \cdot R / V$)
- Stores + water: 2 t
- $W_{DW} \approx 6 + 1.4 + 12 + 2 = 21.4$ t

*Step 5 — Assume lightship is 50–55 % of total for high-speed craft:*
$$\Delta \approx 1.85 \times W_{DW} \approx 39.6 \; t$$
$$\nabla = \Delta / \rho = 39.6 / 1.025 = 38.6 \; m^3$$

*Step 6 — Solve for T:*
$$20.68 \, T^3 = 38.6 \Rightarrow T^3 = 1.867 \Rightarrow T = 1.23 \; m$$

*Step 7 — Compute B and L:*
$$B = 2.8 \times 1.23 = 3.44 \; m$$
$$L = 15.4 \times 1.23 = 18.94 \; m$$

*Step 8 — Compute D:*
$$D = 1.10 \times T = 1.10 \times 1.23 = 1.35 \; m$$

*Step 9 — Check $L_{OA}$:*
$$L_{OA} \approx 1.05 \times 18.94 = 19.9 \; m$$

*Step 10 — Verify C_B:*
$$C_B = \frac{38.6}{18.94 \times 3.44 \times 1.23} = \frac{38.6}{80.2} = 0.481 \;\; \text{✓ matches assumed } 0.48$$

*Step 11 — Verify $L/\nabla^{1/3}$:*
$$\frac{L}{\nabla^{1/3}} = \frac{18.94}{38.6^{1/3}} = \frac{18.94}{3.38} = 5.60$$

This is **low** for semi-displacement (typically 6.0–7.5). Indicates hull is too bluff. **Iterate:** increase $L$ to 22 m by reducing $C_B$ to 0.42 or adding more displacement for fuel.

*Step 12 — Iterated baseline (final answer):*
$$\boxed{L_{WL} = 22 \; m, \quad B = 4.0 \; m, \quad T = 1.43 \; m, \quad D = 1.57 \; m, \quad \Delta = 48 \; t}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternate]**

*(a)* **(7 marks)** Explain in detail the **"Design Spiral"** concept as applied to the planning of a high-speed craft. Draw a labelled schematic showing at least six iterations and explain why a straight-line (sequential) design approach fails.

*(b)* **(7 marks)** A 28 m, 35-knot high-speed ferry is being planned. Estimate the **preliminary Effective Horsepower (EHP)** and **Shaft Horsepower (SHP)** using the following data:

- Displacement $\Delta = 145$ tonnes
- Admiralty coefficient $C_A = 320$
- Hull efficiency $\eta_H = 1.05$
- Propulsive efficiency (rotative + shafting combined) $\eta_R \eta_S = 0.95$
- Gearbox efficiency $\eta_G = 0.98$

Comment on the adequacy of the design for an Indian coastal service with $V = 35$ kn, range 250 nm, payload 60 passengers.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Design Spiral Explanation (7 marks)**

The **Design Spiral** (Andrews, 1954) is the canonical model for naval-architectural planning. It models design as a series of **nested, iterative loops** through the same decision nodes — each pass adds refinement.

**Why a spiral (3 marks):**
- Design variables are **strongly coupled** — you cannot pick $L$ without $\Delta$, and you cannot pick $\Delta$ without $L$.
- Each decision *constrains* the next; only iteration converges to a feasible set.
- Performance, cost, and compliance are **competing objectives** — no single pass can balance all three.

**Six iterations (2 marks):**

| Loop | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mission definition | Operational profile |
| 2 | Hull-form selection | Form family |
| 3 | Main dimensions | $L, B, T, D$ |
| 4 | Displacement estimate | $\Delta$ |
| 5 | Powering estimate | EHP, SHP |
| 6 | Compliance + cost check | Pass/Fail → re-iterate |

**Why sequential design fails (2 marks):**
- A linear sequence (mission → form → dim → power → done) **assumes prior decisions are correct**. If a downstream check (e.g., cost) fails, the entire upstream chain must be redone.
- The spiral allows *concurrent refinement*: dimensions, power, and form are tuned together.
- For high-speed craft, **hump resistance** makes a one-pass sizing infeasible — at least 2–3 passes are needed to avoid the hump.

*Schematic (refers to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1).*

**Part (b) — Powering Estimation (7 marks)**

*Step 1 — Compute EHP from Admiralty coefficient:*
$$C_A = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{EHP}$$

Rearranging:
$$EHP = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{C_A}$$

Compute $\Delta^{2/3}$:
$$\Delta^{2/3} = 145^{2/3} = (145^{1/3})^2 = (5.26)^2 = 27.68$$

Compute $V^3$:
$$V = 35 \text{ kn} \Rightarrow V^3 = 35^3 = 42{,}875$$

Compute EHP:
$$EHP = \frac{27.68 \times 42{,}875}{320} = \frac{1{,}186{,}750}{320} \approx 3{,}708 \; \text{hp}$$

*Step 2 — Compute total propulsive coefficient:*
$$\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S \cdot \eta_G = 1.05 \times 0.95 \times 0.98$$
$$\eta_D = 1.05 \times 0.95 \times 0.98 = 0.978$$

*Step 3 — Compute SHP:*
$$SHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{3{,}708}{0.978} = 3{,}791 \; \text{hp}$$

*Step 4 — Per-passenger power (adequacy check):*
$$\text{Power per passenger} = \frac{3{,}791}{60} = 63.2 \; \text{hp/pax}$$

For Indian coastal service, **30–50 hp/pax is typical**. The craft is **marginally over-powered** — possibly indicating $C_A$ is too high (overly optimistic) or the hull form is not optimised for the speed. *Recommendation:* Review $C_A$ with model tests or CFD; consider a **catamaran** for better payload-to-power ratio.

*Step 5 — Fuel check (informational):*
$$W_{fuel} = \frac{SFC \cdot SHP \cdot R}{V} = \frac{0.22 \times 3{,}791 \times 250}{35} \approx 5{,}958 \; \text{kg} \approx 5.96 \; t$$

This is feasible for a 28 m hull. **Design is acceptable with a hull-form review.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Planning of a Craft**

- [x] **Planning = Preliminary Design** — feasibility sizing, hull-form selection, baseline dimensions, weight, power, cost.
- [x] **Three pillars** of craft design: **Performance ↔ Cost ↔ Compliance** (Design Triangle).
- [x] **Design Spiral** = iterative; **never linear**. Always re-iterate if any check fails.
- [x] **Froude number** $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ is the master discriminator.
- [x] **Speed-Length Ratio (SLR)** $= V_{kn}/\sqrt{L_{ft}} = 1.34 \, F_n$.
- [x] **SLR regimes:** < 0.9 (displacement), 0.9–1.5 (semi-disp), > 1.5 (planing).
- [x] **Planing threshold** = $F_n \approx 0.4$ or $SLR \approx 1.34$ (Savitsky/Blount).
- [x] **Block coefficient** $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$; high-speed craft have $C_B = 0.35 - 0.55$.
- [x] **Admiralty coefficient** $C_A = \Delta^{2/3} V^3 / EHP$ — quick EHP estimator.
- [x] **Hump region** ($F_n \approx 0.25 - 0.40$) is the **resistance maximum** for displacement hulls — avoid in design.
- [x] **Length-displacement ratio** $L/\nabla^{1/3}$ — 5–6 (disp), 6–7.5 (semi-disp), 8+ (catamaran/SES).
- [x] **Slenderness** $L/B$ — 3–4.5 (planing), 4.5–6 (semi-disp), 8+ (catamaran).
- [x] **Seawater density** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1.025 \; t/m^3}$; **$g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$**; **1 kn = 0.5144 m/s**.
- [x] **ITTC-57** is the standard frictional resistance line in planning.
- [x] **$L_{OA} \approx 1.05 \, L_{WL}$** for modern high-speed craft.
- [x] **Watson-Gilfillan / AEGL** statistical lightship weight: $W_{LS} = C_{LS} L^a B^b D^c$.
- [x] **SHP = EHP / $\eta_D$**, with $\eta_D = \eta_H \eta_R \eta_S \eta_G \approx 0.55 - 0.65$ typical.
- [x] **Iterate, iterate, iterate** — first pass almost never converges; KTU examiners reward iterative solutions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Hydrofoil Craft** (also called a *Hydrofoil Supported Craft*) is a high-speed marine vessel in which the hull is lifted clear of the water surface at cruising speeds by means of **hydrofoils** — underwater wing-like lifting surfaces — so that the vessel is supported dynamically by hydrodynamic lift rather than by buoyancy alone.

When the vessel accelerates past its **takeoff speed (V_TO)**, the hydrofoils generate sufficient dynamic lift equal to the weight of the craft, raising the hull above the free water surface. The craft then operates in **foil-borne mode**, where wave-making resistance is dramatically reduced and very high speeds (often 30–60 knots) become achievable.

### 1.2 Operating Modes of a Hydrofoil Craft

A hydrofoil craft typically operates in three distinct regimes:

| Mode | Speed Range | Support Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Displacement Mode** (hull-borne) | $V < V_{TO}$ | Pure buoyancy of the hull |
| **Transition / Takeoff** | $V \approx V_{TO}$ | Mixed buoyancy + partial foil lift (hull may porpoise) |
| **Foil-borne Mode** (airborne hull) | $V > V_{TO}$ | Fully dynamic lift from hydrofoils |

> [!NOTE] **Key Physical Constants Used in Hydrofoil Design**
> - Density of seawater: $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Density of freshwater: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Kinematic viscosity of seawater at 15 °C: $\nu \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - 1 knot $= 0.5144 \text{ m/s}$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Aircraft Wing"

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Analogy**
> Imagine an aeroplane flying through water instead of air. The **hydrofoil** behaves exactly like an aircraft wing — it has a curved upper surface and flatter lower surface, creating a pressure difference that lifts the boat upward. The hull is the "fuselage" dangling in the air, and the **struts** are the "landing gear" connecting the underwater wing to the hull above.

The magic is this: as the craft speeds up, water (which is **~800 times denser than air**) slams against the foils. Even at modest speeds, this dense medium produces enormous lift, allowing a multi-tonne vessel to fly above the waves — which is why hydrofoils were once called *"the only way to fly on water without leaving it."*

### 1.4 Classification of Hydrofoil Craft

Hydrofoil craft are classified on **two independent axes**:

**A. By Foil Geometry (submersion type):**
- **Surface-Piercing Foils (S-Foil / V-Foil):** Foils cut through the water surface; chord partially in air, partially submerged.
- **Fully Submerged Foils:** Entire foil remains underwater at all times; requires active or passive depth control for stability.
- **Variable Geometry Foils:** Foil angle/area can be changed in service.

**B. By Configuration (Foil Arrangement):**
- **Conventional / Tandem (T-Type):** Front foil + rear foil, both fully submerged.
- **Canard:** Only the rear foil is a main lifting foil; the front foil provides trim control.
- **Surface-Piercing (S-Type):** Two V-shaped or U-shaped foils pierce the water; the geometry itself provides stability.
- **J-Type:** Forward canard foil + aft main submerged foil, often used on passenger hydrofoils.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Hydrofoil Lift

The fundamental equation of hydrodynamic lift on a hydrofoil is identical in form to aerodynamic lift — only the fluid density and viscosity are different:

$$L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_{L}$$

Where:
- $L$ = lift force (N)
- $\rho$ = mass density of water (kg/m³)
- $V$ = forward speed of the foil (m/s)
- $S$ = planform area of the foil (m²)
- $C_L$ = lift coefficient (dimensionless, function of angle of attack $\alpha$ and Reynolds number $Re$)

### 2.2 Why Lift = Weight at Takeoff

For the craft to leave the water surface, the lift must equal the total operating weight $W = mg$.

At takeoff:
$$W = L_{TO} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{TO}^{2}\,S\,C_{L,\max}$$

Solving for takeoff speed:

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L,\max}}}$$

### 2.3 Drag on the Hydrofoil

The total drag on a hydrofoil in water has two principal components:

$$D = D_{0} + D_{i} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_{D_{0}} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_{D_{i}}$$

Where:
- $D_0$ = parasitic (profile) drag — independent of lift
- $D_i$ = induced drag — proportional to $C_L^2$, dominant at low speeds
- $C_{D_0}$ = zero-lift drag coefficient
- $C_{D_i}$ = induced drag coefficient $\approx C_L^2/(\pi\,AR\,e)$, with $e$ = Oswald efficiency factor

### 2.4 Aspect Ratio of a Hydrofoil

For a rectangular planform of span $b$ and chord $c$:

$$AR = \dfrac{b^{2}}{S} = \dfrac{b}{c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Design Insight**
> Higher aspect-ratio hydrofoils produce **less induced drag** but are more prone to cavitation and structural bending. A typical cruise hydrofoil has $AR$ between 3 and 6.

### 2.5 Power Required for Foil-Borne Operation

$$P = D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{3}\,S\,C_{D,\text{total}}$$

This shows the dramatic **cubic dependence on speed** — every doubling of speed requires ~8× more power, which is why waterjet propulsion is favored over conventional propellers at high speeds.

### 2.6 Cavitation — The Limiting Phenomenon

At high speeds, the local static pressure on the foil surface may drop below the **vapor pressure** of water, causing vapor bubbles to form — a phenomenon called **cavitation**. Cavitation destroys lift, erodes the foil, and causes vibration.

The **cavitation number** $\sigma$ governs this:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{p_{0} - p_{v}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}}$$

Where:
- $p_0$ = ambient static pressure at foil depth (Pa)
- $p_v$ = vapor pressure of water (~2330 Pa at 20 °C)

Cavitation onset typically begins when $\sigma < 0.6$ for conventional hydrofoils, hence the need for **supercavitating** or **ventilated** foil designs at very high speeds.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_{L}$ | Hydrodynamic lift on hydrofoil |
| 2 | $V_{TO} = \sqrt{2W/(\rho S C_{L,\max})}$ | Takeoff speed condition |
| 3 | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_{D}$ | Total drag on hydrofoil |
| 4 | $AR = b^{2}/S = b/c$ | Aspect ratio (rectangular foil) |
| 5 | $C_{D,i} = C_{L}^{2}/(\pi AR e)$ | Induced drag coefficient |
| 6 | $P = D \cdot V = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{3} S C_{D}$ | Power required (W) |
| 7 | $\sigma = (p_{0} - p_{v})/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2})$ | Cavitation number |
| 8 | $Re = V c / \nu$ | Reynolds number for foil |
| 9 | $T/D = C_L/C_D$ | Aerodynamic efficiency (L/D ratio) |
| 10 | $Fr = V/\sqrt{gL_{WL}}$ | Froude number (hull-borne) |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight**
> The derivation of $V_{TO}$ and the working principles of **surface-piercing** vs **fully submerged** foils are recurrent KTU exam topics and must be memorised with units.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrofoil craft are deployed worldwide in:
- **Passenger ferries** (e.g., Russian *Raketa*, Boeing Jetfoil, Italian *Alis*).
- **Naval fast attack craft** (PHM class Pegasus).
- **Coast guard patrol vessels** for high-speed interception.
- **Oceanographic research platforms** where low wave-induced motion is required.

The principle of *dynamic lift support* also underpins **hydrofoil-assisted catamarans (HYSWAC)**, **surface effect ships**, and modern **air-cavity hulls** — making the hydrofoil concept one of the most influential ideas in 20th-century naval architecture.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Takeoff Speed of a Hydrofoil Craft

**Starting Physical Statement:**
A hydrofoil craft of total operating weight $W$ (N) is supported dynamically by $n$ identical hydrofoils of total planform area $S$ (m²) at a forward speed $V$ (m/s) through water of density $\rho$ (kg/m³). Each foil operates at a maximum lift coefficient $C_{L,\max}$ (dimensionless).

**Step 1 — Hydrodynamic Lift on the Foil System**

The instantaneous lift generated by the hydrofoil system is given by the classical lift equation:

$$L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V^{2}\,S\,C_{L}$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition Just Before Takeoff**

At the precise moment of takeoff, the entire weight of the craft is supported by hydrodynamic lift, and the buoyancy contribution from the hull becomes negligible. Applying vertical equilibrium:

$$\sum F_{V} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{TO} - W = 0$$

$$\therefore \quad L_{TO} = W$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the Lift Equation at Takeoff**

At takeoff, the speed is $V = V_{TO}$ and the lift coefficient reaches its maximum allowable value $C_{L,\max}$ to minimise the required speed. Substituting into the lift equation:

$$W = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{TO}^{2}\,S\,C_{L,\max}$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $V_{TO}$**

Rearranging for $V_{TO}^{2}$:

$$V_{TO}^{2} = \dfrac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L,\max}}$$

Taking the positive square root (physical solution):

$$\boxed{\,V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho\,S\,C_{L,\max}}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation**

- Increasing **weight** $W$ requires a **higher** takeoff speed.
- Increasing **foil area** $S$ or **$C_{L,\max}$** reduces the takeoff speed.
- The square-root dependence shows that doubling the foil area cuts $V_{TO}$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ only.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example**

A hydrofoil craft weighs $W = 200{,}000$ N in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). The combined foil area is $S = 8$ m² and the maximum lift coefficient is $C_{L,\max} = 1.2$. Find the takeoff speed.

$$V_{TO} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 200{,}000}{1025 \times 8 \times 1.2}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{400{,}000}{9840}} = \sqrt{40.65}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{TO} \approx 6.38 \text{ m/s} \approx 12.39 \text{ knots}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Lift at a Given Speed

**Starting Point:** Once airborne, the same foil supports weight at the cruise speed $V_c$ with a *lower* $C_L$ than at takeoff. From equilibrium:

$$L_c = W = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_{c}^{2}\,S\,C_{L,c}$$

**Rearranging for $C_{L,c}$:**

$$C_{L,c} = \dfrac{2W}{\rho\,V_{c}^{2}\,S}$$

This formula is **inversely proportional to $V_c^2$** — a key insight: as the craft accelerates past takeoff, the required angle of attack (and therefore $C_L$) must be **reduced** to prevent over-lift and to maintain trim.

### 3.3 Derivation of Froude Number at Takeoff

A useful dimensionless speed parameter for hydrofoil craft at takeoff is the **takeoff Froude number**:

$$Fr_{TO} = \dfrac{V_{TO}}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$$

Where $L_{WL}$ is the waterline length of the hull (m). The transition from displacement to foil-borne typically occurs at $Fr_{TO} \approx 1.0$ to $1.4$ for well-designed craft.

### 3.4 Analytic Comparison Matrix — Foil Types

| Parameter | Surface-Piercing (S-Foil) | Fully Submerged (T-Foil) |
|---|---|---|
| Stability | **Passive** (chord-length variation provides restoring moment) | **Active / controlled** (requires flaps or depth control) |
| Takeoff behaviour | Gradual, smooth | Can be abrupt; risk of broaching |
| Sea-state sensitivity | Lower — geometry adapts to waves | Higher — needs rapid control response |
| Complexity | Mechanical simplicity, fewer parts | Hydraulic actuators, sensors, control system |
| Cavitation tendency | Lower (operates near free surface) | Higher (depth pressure suppresses cavitation) |
| Typical applications | Small fast craft, naval hydrofoils | Passenger ferries, high-speed cruise |

### 3.5 Numerical Example — Lift at Cruise Speed

Continuing from the previous example, what is the lift coefficient required to maintain cruise at $V_c = 30$ knots?

Convert speed: $V_c = 30 \times 0.5144 = 15.43$ m/s.

$$C_{L,c} = \dfrac{2 \times 200{,}000}{1025 \times (15.43)^{2} \times 8} = \dfrac{400{,}000}{1{,}951{,}560} \approx 0.205$$

This is well below $C_{L,\max} = 1.2$, leaving a large **lift margin** to absorb gusts, wave impacts, and manoeuvring loads.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hydrofoil Craft

The diagram below maps the **interactions** between the principal subsystems of a typical fully-submerged hydrofoil craft (J-type configuration), including the major force flows.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hull and Superstructure] --> B[Forward Canard Foil Strut]
    A --> C[Aft Main Lifting Foil Strut]
    B --> D[Forward Submerged Hydrofoil]
    C --> E[Aft Submerged Hydrofoil]
    D --> F[Water Free Surface]
    E --> F
    A --> G[Waterjet Propulsion Unit]
    G --> F
    F --> H[Hull Displacement Drag Stage Below VTO]
    F --> I[Foil Borne Lift Stage Above VTO]
    D --> J[Active Flap Control System]
    E --> J
    J --> K[Hydraulic Actuator Bank]
    K --> L[Onboard Gyro and Accelerometer Sensors]
    L --> J
    A --> M[Onboard Diesel or Gas Turbine Engine]
    M --> G
    M --> N[Marine Gearbox]
    N --> G
    O[Operator Control Console] --> J
    O --> G
    P[Trim and Heave Recovery System] --> J
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **hull** $A$ is the structural backbone, supported in air above the free surface $F$.
- Two **struts** $B, C$ transmit the dynamic lift from the underwater foils $D, E$ to the hull.
- A **waterjet unit** $G$ provides thrust, drawing its water from below and exhausting above.
- The **active flap control system** $J$ regulates the angle of attack of the submerged foils using feedback from gyros and accelerometers $L$.
- This feedback loop is what keeps a **fully submerged** hydrofoil stable — a key KTU exam point of contrast with passive surface-piercing designs.

### 4.2 Force Diagram at Foil-Borne Condition

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Forces
        W[Weight W acting downward at CG]
        L1[Lift on Front Foil L1]
        L2[Lift on Rear Foil L2]
        T[Thrust T at waterjet outlet]
        D1[Drag on Front Foil D1]
        D2[Drag on Rear Foil D2]
        Dh[Aerodynamic Drag on Hull Dh]
    end
    L1 --> Craft[Hydrofoil Craft in Foil Borne Mode]
    L2 --> Craft
    T --> Craft
    D1 --> Craft
    D2 --> Craft
    Dh --> Craft
    W --> Craft
```

> [!NOTE] **Equilibrium Conditions**
> - **Vertical:** $L_1 + L_2 = W$
> - **Horizontal:** $T = D_1 + D_2 + D_h$
> - **Pitch (moment about CG):** $L_1 \cdot x_1 = L_2 \cdot x_2$ where $x_1, x_2$ are longitudinal distances from CG.

### 4.3 Foil Geometry Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hydrofoil Craft Classification] --> B[By Foil Submersion]
    A --> C[By Configuration]
    B --> D[Surface Piercing S Foil]
    B --> E[Fully Submerged T Foil]
    B --> F[Variable Geometry Foil]
    C --> G[Conventional Tandem]
    C --> H[Canard J Type]
    C --> I[Surface Piercing S Type]
    D --> J[Passive Stability via Chord Variation]
    E --> K[Active Stability via Flap Control]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]** *(3 Marks)*

**Q1. Define a hydrofoil craft. List any three advantages of foil-borne operation over displacement mode.**

**Model Answer:**
A **hydrofoil craft** is a marine vessel that uses underwater wing-shaped surfaces (hydrofoils) to generate hydrodynamic lift, raising the hull above the water surface at cruising speeds. Once foil-borne, the craft is supported dynamically by lift rather than by buoyancy.

**Three advantages:**
1. **Dramatic reduction in wave-making resistance** — only the foils touch water, not the hull.
2. **Higher achievable speed** — speeds of 35–60 knots are common, limited mainly by cavitation and power.
3. **Improved passenger comfort** — hull is decoupled from wave action, reducing motion sickness in seaway.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]** *(3 Marks)*

**Q2. Differentiate between surface-piercing and fully submerged hydrofoils with respect to stability and control.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Surface-Piercing Foil | Fully Submerged Foil |
|---|---|---|
| Stability mechanism | **Passive** — geometry of foil below waterline provides self-stabilising moment | **Active** — requires flaps, sensors, hydraulic actuators |
| Control complexity | Simple, mechanical | Sophisticated, electronic-hydraulic |
| Sea-state tolerance | Excellent | Sensitive to rapid wave forcing |
| Risk of abrupt takeoff | Low | Higher |
| Typical use | Small craft, military | Passenger ferries, cruise hydrofoils |

---
