            &= \sqrt{\frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 14}{1.64 \times 10^6 + 0.087 \times 10^6}} \\
            &= \sqrt{\frac{140{,}764}{1.727 \times 10^6}} \\
            &= \sqrt{0.0815} \\
            &\approx 0.286\,\text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}$$

The corresponding period:

$$T_{n,z} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_{n,z}} = \frac{2\pi}{0.286} \approx 22.0\,\text{s}$$

This is **much longer** than typical wave periods of 5–10 s → SWATH operates **below resonance** in heave (sub-resonant regime) → **excellent seakeeping**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pitch Equation of Motion (Conceptual)

For small pitch angle $\theta$ about the centre of gravity, the rigid-body equation is:

$$(I_{yy} + I_{a,yy})\ddot{\theta} + B_\theta \dot{\theta} + \rho g I_{T} \,\theta = M_{wave}(t)$$

where:
- $I_{yy}$ = mass moment of inertia about transverse axis through CG
- $I_{a,yy}$ = added inertia in pitch
- $I_{T} = \int_{WPL} x^2 \, dA$ = transverse second moment of waterplane area
- $B_\theta$ = pitch damping
- $M_{wave}$ = wave-exciting moment

For a SWATH with two widely spaced struts of area $A_s$ each, separated laterally by $2b$:

$$I_T \approx 2 \times A_s \times b^2$$

Because $b \approx 0.25 L$, even a small $A_s$ produces a **large $I_T$** → pitch stiffness is high → pitch natural frequency is well above wave frequencies.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Monohull | Catamaran | SWATH |
|---|---|---|---|
| Waterplane area | Large | Moderate (2 demi-hulls) | Very small (struts only) |
| Heave freq. vs. wave freq. | Near resonance | Near resonance | **Below resonance** |
| Pitch stability | Moderate | Good | **Excellent** |
| Resistance at speed | Low | Low–Moderate | **High** (+20–40 %) |
| Payload in lower hull | Yes | Limited | Not directly usable |
| Structural complexity | Low | Low | **High** (joint fatigue) |
| Operability in SS6+ | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SWATH Vessel Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wave Excitation Force F_wave] --> B[Surface Piercing Struts]
    B --> C[Strut Waterplane Area A_WP]
    C --> D[Hydrostatic + Hydrodynamic Loads]
    D --> E[Cross Structure / Upper Hull Deck]
    E --> F[Payload Cabin and Equipment]
    G[Submerged Demi Hull Left] --> H[Buoyancy Volume nabla_L]
    I[Submerged Demi Hull Right] --> J[Buoyancy Volume nabla_R]
    H --> K[Static and Dynamic Pressure]
    J --> K
    K --> E
    L[Shaft Line in Strut] --> M[Propulsion Unit]
    N[Rudder or Waterjet] --> O[Directional Control]
    M --> L
```

## 4.2 SWATH Motion Response Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Incoming Wave] --> Q[Force on Strut Only]
    Q --> R[Small Added Mass m_a]
    R --> S[High Heave Natural Frequency omega_n]
    S --> T[Sub-Resonant Response]
    T --> U[Low Motion Amplitude at Deck]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```
+--------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  UPPER HULL (CROSS DECK)                     |
|   Payload Cabin | Crew Spaces | Helipad / Sensors            |
+======================+=======================================+
                       ||
                       ||  <-- STRUTS (Surface Piercing)
                       ||       - Small waterplane area A_WP
                       ||       - Wave load entry point
                       ||       - Slamming load region
                       ||
+----------------------+---------------------------------------+
|   LEFT DEMI-HULL     |       RIGHT DEMI-HULL                 |
|   (Submerged)        |       (Submerged)                     |
|                      |                                       |
|   Streamlined form   |       Streamlined form                 |
|   Provides buoyancy  |       Provides buoyancy               |
|   Carries bending    |       Carries bending                 |
|   Shaft line inside  |       Shaft line inside               |
+----------------------+---------------------------------------+
```

## 4.4 Seakeeping Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Monohull: Large A_WP] --> M2[Low Heave Frequency]
    M2 --> M3[Resonance in Waves]
    M3 --> M4[Large Heave Motion]

    C1[Catamaran: Moderate A_WP] --> C2[Mid Heave Frequency]
    C2 --> C3[Near Resonance]
    C3 --> C4[Moderate Heave Motion]

    S1[SWATH: Tiny A_WP] --> S2[High Heave Frequency]
    S2 --> S3[Below Resonance in Operating Range]
    S3 --> S4[Small Heave Motion]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Define a SWATH vessel and state its defining geometric property.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A SWATH (Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull) vessel is a high-speed craft consisting of **two fully submerged lower hulls (demi-hulls)** connected to an elevated **upper hull** by means of **narrow vertical struts**. Its defining geometric property is the **deliberate reduction of waterplane area to that of the struts only**, with the bulk of displacement volume held below the wave-action layer. (3 marks)

### Question A2
**Why does a SWATH exhibit superior seakeeping compared with a monohull of the same displacement?**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The waterplane area $A_{WP}$ is restricted to the struts → **low added mass in heave** → **high heave natural frequency** → resonance lies above the dominant wave-energy band. (1.5 marks)
2. The buoyancy is held deep underwater, so wave excitation forces act only on the struts → **smaller exciting force and moment**. (1 mark)
3. The widely spaced struts provide a **large transverse second moment of waterplane area**, giving high pitch stiffness. (0.5 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Each 14-mark question typically carries sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks.*

---

### Question B1 (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **structural arrangement** of a SWATH vessel and explain the **role of each major component** in contributing to its seakeeping performance. **\[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **equation of motion in heave** for a SWATH vessel in regular waves and show that the natural frequency is given by $\omega_{n,z} = \sqrt{\rho g A_{WP}/(m+m_a)}$. Discuss how the small waterplane area shifts the resonance away from the wave-frequency band. **\[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Component | Description | Seakeeping Role | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Demi-hull (lower) | Streamlined, fully submerged | Provides buoyancy, lowers metacentric vulnerability, isolates payload from wave action | 2.0 |
| Strut | Narrow vertical surface-piercing member | Provides *only* dynamic waterplane area; controls added mass and damping | 2.0 |
| Cross structure (upper hull) | Transverse deck box | Distributes loads, houses payload, contributes pitch stiffness through strut separation | 1.5 |
| Appendages | Shaft, propeller, rudder inside or behind strut | Minimise drag, avoid exciting surface waves | 1.0 |
| Schematic | Neatly labelled front and side views | Award | 0.5 |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Vertical force balance on a heaving vessel (D'Alembert):

$$(m + m_a)\ddot{z} + B\dot{z} + \rho g A_{WP}\,z = F_0 \cos(\omega_e t)$$

**Step 2:** Identify natural frequency:

$$\omega_{n,z} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho g A_{WP}}{m + m_a}}$$

**Step 3:** Because $A_{WP}$ in a SWATH is 5–10 % of a monohull, the numerator $\rho g A_{WP}$ is small. However, the **added mass** $m_a$ (which also scales with $A_{WP}$ through the strut slenderness) drops proportionally — but **disproportionately less**, because strut-added mass grows only as $\pi a^2 L_s$, not as the demi-hull planform. Net effect: $\omega_{n,z}$ lies in the **sub-resonant regime** for typical wave frequencies (0.5–1.0 rad/s). (3 marks)

**Step 4:** Compute numerical illustration for typical SWATH (use values from §3.1): $\omega_{n,z} \approx 0.286$ rad/s, $T_n \approx 22$ s — clearly outside wave-period band. (1 mark)

---

### Question B2 (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the **seakeeping characteristics, resistance behaviour, and structural complexity** of SWATH vessels with monohulls and catamarans. Use a comparative table and discuss the operational trade-offs. **\[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A SWATH vessel has two struts of circular cross-section, each of diameter 3 m and wetted length 6 m. Demi-hull displacement per side is 800 m³. Estimate the **heave added mass** and the **heave natural frequency**. Take seawater density $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$. **\[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

A comparative table (4 marks) covering: waterplane area, heave resonance behaviour, pitch stiffness, resistance penalty, payload usability in lower hull, structural complexity, and operability in SS6/SS7.

Discussion of trade-offs (3 marks):
- **Advantage of SWATH:** Operability in high seas; payload protected below waterline; helicopter deck usable in high SS.
- **Disadvantage:** 20–40 % higher resistance at cruise; submerged volume not directly usable; strut-demihull joint fatigue-critical; complex shafting.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Strut diameter $2a = 3\,\text{m}$, so $a = 1.5\,\text{m}$
- Wetted length $L_s = 6\,\text{m}$
- Two struts
- Displacement per demi-hull = $800\,\text{m}^3$

**Step 1 — Total Mass:**
$$m = \rho \nabla_{\text{total}} = 1025 \times (2 \times 800) = 1.64 \times 10^6\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Added Mass (Slender-Strut Approximation):**
$$m_a = 2 \rho \pi a^2 L_s = 2 \times 1025 \times \pi \times (1.5)^2 \times 6 \approx 86{,}948\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Effective Strut Waterplane Area:**
For two circular struts: $A_{WP} = 2 \times \pi a^2 = 2 \times \pi \times 2.25 \approx 14.14\,\text{m}^2$

**Step 4 — Heave Natural Frequency:**
$$\omega_{n,z} = \sqrt{\frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 14.14}{1.64 \times 10^6 + 0.087 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{0.0816} \approx 0.286\,\text{rad/s}$$

$$T_{n,z} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_{n,z}} \approx 22\,\text{s}$$

**Conclusion:** Heave natural period (22 s) lies far above typical wave periods (5–10 s), confirming the sub-resonant, low-motion behaviour of the SWATH. (1 mark for interpretation)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing waterplane area with demi-hull planform area.** Students often write "the demi-hulls have small waterplane area" — this is wrong; the demi-hulls are submerged and have *no* waterplane. The **struts** provide the waterplane area. (KTU examiners deduct full marks for this confusion.)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the added-mass term $m_a$.** Many answers quote $\omega_n = \sqrt{\rho g A_{WP}/m}$, omitting the added mass. For slender struts, $m_a$ is ~5 % of $m$ — small but *must be shown in the equation*.
>
> 3. **Confusing heave and pitch natural frequencies.** Heave uses $A_{WP}$; pitch uses $I_T$ (second moment of waterplane area). Mixing the two loses 2 marks easily.
>
> 4. **Not labelling the schematic.** A neat, fully labelled SWATH cross-section is worth 1–2 marks by itself. Skipping it loses easy marks.
>
> 5. **Stating "SWATH is faster" without justification.** SWATH is *not* primarily a fast vessel; it is a **stable** vessel in rough seas. State the correct advantage (seakeeping, operability) — not "speed".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **SWATH = Small Waterplane Area Twin Hull** — twin submerged demi-hulls connected by struts to an upper deck.
- **Waterplane area** is **only the struts' cross-section**, not the demi-hulls.
- **Buoyancy** is provided by the **submerged demi-hulls**, kept deep below the wave layer.
- The defining hydrodynamic benefit: **reduced added mass + reduced exciting force** → high heave natural frequency → **sub-resonant operation** → excellent seakeeping.
- **Heave equation:** $(m + m_a)\ddot{z} + B\dot{z} + \rho g A_{WP} z = F_{wave}(t)$.
- **Heave natural frequency:** $\omega_{n,z} = \sqrt{\rho g A_{WP}/(m+m_a)}$.
- **Pitch natural frequency:** $\omega_{n,\theta} = \sqrt{\rho g I_T/(I_{yy}+I_{a,yy})}$ where $I_T \approx 2 A_s b^2$.
- **Trade-off:** superior seakeeping comes at the cost of **20–40 % higher resistance** and **structural complexity** at the strut-demihull joint.
- **Applications:** naval surveillance, oceanographic research, passenger ferries, offshore patrol.
- **Slender-strut added mass:** $m_a = 2 \rho \pi a^2 L_s$ (per pair).
- **Slenderness rule:** strut $L/D \geq 4$ to maintain slender-body approximation.
- **Demi-hull depth:** typically $T \approx 0.5 L$ to escape wave excitation.
- **KTU tip:** Always draw a labelled SWATH schematic in 7-mark and 14-mark answers — it is high-reward, low-effort.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unconventional Hulls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST745 - DESIGN OF HIGH SPEED CRAFTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unconventional Hulls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unconventional Hulls** are hull forms whose primary support mechanism against gravity deviates fundamentally from the classical Archimedean buoyancy principle that governs conventional displacement hulls. In unconventional hull forms, a significant portion (or entirety) of the craft's weight is supported by **dynamic lift** generated through hydrodynamic or aerodynamic pressure differentials, or by pressurized gas cushions.

In high-speed craft design, the KTU 2024 scheme classifies unconventional hulls into the following canonical categories:

1. **Planing Hulls** — Hulls that rise dynamically out of the water at speed, supported by hydrodynamic lift on flat or V-shaped running surfaces.
2. **Hydrofoils** — Vessels supported entirely (or partially) by submerged or surface-piercing foil systems that generate hydrodynamic lift.
3. **Air Cushion Vehicles (ACV / Hovercraft)** — Craft supported by a pressurized air cushion trapped between the hull and the water/ground surface.
4. **Surface Effect Ships (SES)** — A hybrid between ACV and catamaran, with rigid sidewalls containing a pressurized air cushion.
5. **Multi-Hull Forms (Catamarans, SWATH, Trimaran)** — Configurations using two or more slender hulls to reduce wave-making resistance while maintaining buoyancy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a conventional ship as a **floating cork** — it floats because the water it displaces weighs as much as the ship itself (Archimedes' principle). At low speeds, this works beautifully. But try to push the cork faster, and it slaps against the water surface, creating enormous waves that drag it back. Now imagine you **tilt the cork** so that it skims across the water like a skipping stone — most of its weight is now carried by the **impact force** (dynamic lift) of water slamming against its angled bottom, not by floating. This is exactly what planing hulls do.
>
> Now imagine you attach **underwater wings (hydrofoils)** beneath the cork so that as it moves forward, water flowing over the wings lifts the entire craft clear of the surface. The cork now flies through water instead of through waves. This is the hydrofoil principle.
>
> Finally, imagine blowing a powerful cushion of air beneath the cork so it floats on a **pillow of pressurized air** — no water contact needed for support. This is the hovercraft.

## 1.3 Why Unconventional Hulls Exist — The Physical Trigger

At conventional Froude numbers, displacement hulls become hopelessly inefficient due to a cubic relationship between wave-making resistance and speed. The **hull speed barrier** is well-known: a displacement vessel's speed is effectively capped at approximately **1.34 × √LWL** (in knots), where LWL is the waterline length.

**Critical metrics used to classify speed regimes:**
- **Froude Number:** $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$ (dimensionless)
- **Speed-Length Ratio:** $SLR = \dfrac{V_{knots}}{\sqrt{L_{ft}}}$ (commonly used in planing hull design)
- **Block Coefficient:** $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ (displacement hull efficiency)

Unconventional hulls become economically and physically necessary when mission requirements demand operational speeds where $F_n > 0.6$ or $SLR > 5.0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the **physical justification** for unconventional hull forms. The correct answer is: *to overcome the resistance hump and hull-speed barrier inherent in displacement hulls by introducing dynamic support mechanisms.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Physics — The Resistance Problem

The total resistance of any marine craft can be decomposed as:

$$R_T = R_F + R_R + R_W + R_A + R_B$$

Where:
- $R_F$ = Frictional resistance (skin friction)
- $R_R$ = Residuary resistance (wave-making + wave-breaking)
- $R_W$ = Wave-making resistance
- $R_A$ = Aerodynamic resistance (above-water hull)
- $R_B$ = Appendage resistance (foils, struts, rudders)

> [!NOTE]
> For **conventional displacement hulls**, $R_W$ grows approximately as $V^6$ (proportional to the **fourth power of Froude number**), making high speeds energetically prohibitive. This is the fundamental reason unconventional hulls exist.

For **planing hulls**, the dominant resistance becomes **frictional and spray-making** rather than wave-making, allowing much higher speeds at acceptable power levels.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of Unconventional Hull Support

### Pillar 1: Planing Hulls
- Support arises from **dynamic pressure** on flat or V-shaped planing surfaces.
- At high speed, the hull rides up on its bow wave, increasing the dynamic angle of attack.
- Key parameters: **deadrise angle ($\beta$)**, **longitudinal center of gravity (LCG)**, **trim angle ($\tau$)**.

### Pillar 2: Hydrofoils
- Support arises from **circulatory lift** on submerged or surface-piercing wings.
- Lift equation (analogous to aerodynamics): $L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$
- Foils can be **fully submerged**, **surface-piercing**, or **partially submerged**.

### Pillar 3: Air Cushion Vehicles (ACV)
- Support arises from **aerostatic pressure** of a confined air cushion.
- Cushion pressure must equal the weight per unit area: $P_c = \dfrac{W}{A_c}$

### Pillar 4: Multi-Hull Configurations
- Slender demi-hulls reduce wave interference while providing large deck area.
- Catamarans retain buoyancy support but offer reduced wave-making resistance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Description | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude Number (speed-length scaling) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A + R_B$ | Total resistance decomposition | N |
| 3 | $L_{foil} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$ | Hydrofoil lift (per foil) | N |
| 4 | $P_c = \dfrac{W}{A_c}$ | Cushion pressure requirement | Pa |
| 5 | $\tan\tau = \dfrac{R - R_B}{W - W_B}$ | Planing hull trim angle equilibrium | degrees |
| 6 | $C_L = 2\pi \alpha$ (thin foil theory) | 2D lift coefficient for low angles | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\nabla_{displ} = \dfrac{W}{\rho g}$ | Displaced volume required (Archimedes) | m³ |
| 8 | $SLR = \dfrac{V_{kts}}{\sqrt{L_{ft}}}$ | Speed-Length Ratio (planing regime) | kts / √ft |
| 9 | $V_{hull-speed} \approx 1.34\sqrt{L_{ft}}$ | Hull-speed barrier (displacement) | knots |
| 10 | $\beta = \text{angle of V-bottom from horizontal}$ | Deadrise angle | degrees |
| 11 | $P_{installed} = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_H}$ | Required installed power | W |
| 12 | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC frictional coefficient | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note:** The ITTC frictional line applies to turbulent boundary layers on smooth hulls; planing surfaces often exhibit mixed laminar-turbulent flow and require modified formulations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Military Fast Attack Craft:** Use planing hulls (e.g., Mark V Special Operations Craft) for 40+ knot speeds.
- **Commercial Ferries:** Use hydrofoils (Boeing Jetfoil, Russian Raketa) and catamarans (Incat, Austal).
- **Offshore Supply:** Use SWATH forms for stability in heavy seas.
- **Amphibious Assault:** Use hovercraft (LCAC) for beach-landing operations.
- **Naval Aviation Integration:** SES used in the US Navy's SES-200 design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each unconventional form represents a different **trade-off** between speed, seakeeping, payload, and structural complexity. Planing hulls offer simplicity but poor seakeeping; hydrofoils offer excellent efficiency but limited seakeeping in heavy weather; ACVs offer amphibious capability but high fuel consumption; multi-hulls balance efficiency and stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Froude Number as a Scaling Parameter

**Starting principle:** Wave-making resistance depends on the *pattern* of waves generated, which in turn depends on the ratio of inertial to gravitational forces at the bow.

**Step 1 — Dimensional Analysis Setup**
Let the wave-making resistance $R_W$ depend on:
- Velocity $V$ [LT⁻¹]
- Length $L$ [L]
- Gravity $g$ [LT⁻²]
- Density $\rho$ [ML⁻³]

The dimensions of $R_W$ are [MLT⁻²].

**Step 2 — Form the dimensionless group**
By Buckingham Pi theorem, the wave resistance coefficient is a function of Froude number:
$$C_{W} = \dfrac{R_W}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S} = f(F_n)$$

This is the empirical backbone of model testing.

## 3.2 Derivation: Planing Hull Force Equilibrium (Slender Body Approach)

Consider a 2D planing surface at trim angle $\tau$ moving with velocity $V$.

**Step 1 — Dynamic pressure on planing surface**
The stagnation (dynamic) pressure on a wetted element is:
$$p = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$$

**Step 2 — Pressure distribution along the wetted length**
Integrating the dynamic pressure over the wetted chine length $\ell$ (per unit beam):
$$N = \int_0^\ell p \, dx = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \cdot \ell$$

**Step 3 — Vertical lift component**
The vertical (support) component is obtained by multiplying by $\cos\tau$:
$$L = N \cos\tau = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 \ell \cos\tau$$

**Step 4 — Trim equilibrium**
For static equilibrium at constant trim, the moment about the bow must vanish. This yields the **Savitsky equation** (simplified form):
$$C_{L_\beta} = \beta^{0.6} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{1 + \lambda}\right)^{0.8} \cdot \tau^{0.6}$$

Where:
- $\beta$ = deadrise angle (radians)
- $\lambda$ = $\ell / b$ (mean wetted length-beam ratio)
- $\tau$ = trim angle (radians)

> [!NOTE]
> The full Savitsky formula (1964) is the **gold standard** for planing hull resistance prediction and is a high-yield KTU topic.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cushion Pressure for a Hovercraft

**Step 1 — Weight-to-area relation**
The cushion must support the full weight $W$ over its effective planform area $A_c$:
$$P_c \cdot A_c = W$$

**Step 2 — Solve for cushion pressure**
$$P_c = \dfrac{W}{A_c}$$

**Step 3 — Fan power requirement**
The volumetric flow rate $Q$ needed to maintain the cushion against leakage $Q_L$ is supplied by lift fans. Power requirement:
$$P_{fan} = \dfrac{P_c \cdot Q}{\eta_{fan}}$$

**Step 4 — Effective hover height**
For a circular cushion of radius $R$ with gap height $h$:
$$Q_L \approx \pi R \cdot h \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2P_c}{\rho_{air}}}$$

This leakage determines how much air the fans must supply continuously.

## 3.4 Derivation: Hydrofoil Lift (Thin Aerofoil Theory)

**Step 1 — Apply Kutta-Joukowski theorem**
$$L' = \rho V \Gamma$$

where $\Gamma$ is bound circulation, $L'$ is lift per unit span.

**Step 2 — Relate circulation to angle of attack**
For thin airfoil at small $\alpha$:
$$\Gamma = \pi c V \alpha$$

**Step 3 — Substitute to obtain section lift**
$$L' = \pi \rho V^2 c \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Express as lift coefficient**
$$C_L = \dfrac{L'}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 c} = 2\pi \alpha$$

**Step 5 — Total foil lift**
Integrating along span $b$ with span efficiency $\eta_s$:
$$L_{foil} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L \eta_s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For 3D foils of finite aspect ratio, induced drag becomes significant. The actual $C_L$ is lower than $2\pi\alpha$ due to **downwash** and **tip vortex** effects.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Hull Type | Primary Support | Froude Range | Key Advantage | Key Limitation |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|---------------|----------------|
| Displacement | Buoyancy | $F_n < 0.4$ | Excellent seakeeping | Hull-speed barrier |
| Semi-displacement | Buoyancy + partial lift | $0.4 < F_n < 0.6$ | Higher speed than displacement | Resistance hump transition |
| Planing | Dynamic lift | $F_n > 1.0$ (SLR > 5) | High speed | Slamming, poor seakeeping |
| Hydrofoil | Hydrodynamic lift | $F_n > 1.0$ | Low resistance | Foil-strike risk, limited payload |
| ACV (Hovercraft) | Aerostatic pressure | All speeds | Amphibious | High fuel burn, low payload |
| SES | Aerostatic + buoyancy | All speeds | Better efficiency than ACV | Complex skirt systems |
| Catamaran | Buoyancy (split) | $0.3 < F_n < 0.7$ | Large deck area | Resonance synchronism |
| SWATH | Buoyancy (submerged) | $0.3 < F_n < 0.5$ | Excellent stability | Higher resistance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Hierarchy of Unconventional Hulls

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[UNCONVENTIONAL HULLS] --> B0[Dynamic Lift Type]
    A0 --> C0[Aerostatic Lift Type]
    A0 --> D0[Buoyancy Type Modified]

    B0 --> B1[Planing Hulls]
    B0 --> B2[Hydrofoils]
    B0 --> B3[Stepped Hulls]

    C0 --> C1[Air Cushion Vehicles]
    C0 --> C2[Surface Effect Ships]

    D0 --> D1[Catamarans]
    D0 --> D2[SWATH]
    D0 --> D3[Trimaran]
    D0 --> D4[Semi Displacement]

    B1 --> B1A[Flat Bottom]
    B1 --> B1B[V Bottom Deadrise]
    B2 --> B2A[Surface Piercing]
    B2 --> B2B[Fully Submerged]
    C1 --> C1A[Skirted ACV]
    C1 --> C1B[Plenum Chamber ACV]

    classDef primary fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    classDef secondary fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    classDef tertiary fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    class A0 primary
    class B0,C0,D0 secondary
    class B1,B2,B3,C1,C2,D1,D2,D3,D4 tertiary
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Support Mechanisms

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[GRAVITATIONAL LOAD W] --> B0[SUPPORT MECHANISM SELECTOR]
    B0 --> C0[HULL TYPE A PLANING]
    B0 --> D0[HULL TYPE B HYDROFOIL]
    B0 --> E0[HULL TYPE C ACV]
    B0 --> F0[HULL TYPE D MULTIHULL]

    C0 --> C1[Hydrodynamic Pressure on Wetted Surface]
    C1 --> C2[Dynamic Lift equals W]

    D0 --> D1[Submerged Foils Generate Circulation]
    D1 --> D2[Kutta Joukowski Lift equals W]

    E0 --> E1[Pressurized Air Cushion via Lift Fan]
    E1 --> E2[Cushion Pressure times Area equals W]

    F0 --> F1[Demi Hulls provide Buoyancy]
    F1 --> F2[Archimedes Displacement equals W]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef typebox fill:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    classDef mechbox fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    classDef resultbox fill:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    class A0 root
    class B0 typebox
    class C0,D0,E0,F0 typebox
    class C1,D1,E1,F1 mechbox
    class C2,D2,E2,F2 resultbox
```

## 4.3 Resistance Component Dominance Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[TOTAL RESISTANCE R subscript T] --> B0[Frictional R subscript F]
    A0 --> C0[Wave Making R subscript W]
    A0 --> D0[Aerodynamic R subscript A]
    A0 --> E0[Appendage R subscript B]

    B0 --> B1[Always Present]
    B0 --> B2[Planing Hull Dominant]

    C0 --> C1[Displacement Dominant at Low F subscript n]
    C0 --> C2[Diminishes for Planing]

    D0 --> D1[ACV Dominant]
    D0 --> D2[Hydrofoil Dominant]

    E0 --> E1[Hydrofoil Strut Drag]
    E0 --> E2[ACV Sidewall Drag]

    classDef hub fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef comp fill:#854d0e,color:#ffffff
    classDef detail fill:#374151,color:#ffffff
    class A0 hub
    class B0,C0,D0,E0 comp
    class B1,B2,C1,C2,D1,D2,E1,E2 detail
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each unconventional hull form corresponds to a unique *dominant resistance component*. Planing hulls are dominated by $R_F$ (friction); displacement hulls by $R_W$ (wave-making); ACVs by $R_A$ (aerodynamic + skirt drag); hydrofoils by $R_B$ (strut drag) plus induced drag.

## 4.4 Force Diagram — Planing Hull Equilibrium

```
                    F_L (Lift)
                       ↑
                       |
                       |
                  +---------+
                 /  ∡ τ    /  ← Bottom planing surface
                +---------+
                \         /   ← Deadrise angle β
                 \       /
                  +-----+
                       |
                       ↓
                    F_W (Weight)
```

**Equilibrium Conditions:**
- Vertical: $F_L \cos\tau = F_W$
- Horizontal: $F_L \sin\tau = R_{total}$

These two equations together solve for both $\tau$ and required speed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Define unconventional hulls and explain the physical reason for their existence in high-speed craft design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Unconventional hulls are hull forms that derive support not solely from Archimedean buoyancy, but from dynamic lift (planing, hydrofoils), aerostatic pressure (ACVs), or modified buoyancy distribution (multi-hulls). They are necessary because at high Froude numbers ($F_n > 0.6$), conventional displacement hulls encounter a resistance hump due to wave-making resistance scaling with the fourth power of Froude number. This creates a **hull-speed barrier** beyond which displacement operation is energetically prohibitive. Unconventional hulls bypass this barrier by changing the fundamental support mechanism, allowing efficient operation at $F_n > 1.0$ and SLR $> 5.0$.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
**List any four types of unconventional hulls and state one operational advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 marks per item):**
1. **Planing hull** — High top speed (40+ knots); simple mechanical structure.
2. **Hydrofoil** — Low resistance at cruise; can operate above wave-induced motion.
3. **Air Cushion Vehicle (Hovercraft)** — Amphibious capability; beach-landing possible.
4. **Catamaran** — Large deck area and stability; good for passenger ferries.
5. **SWATH** — Excellent seakeeping in heavy seas (mention any 4).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam — Past Year Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain with neat sketches the working principle of planing hulls. Derive the basic trim equilibrium equations for a planing hull operating at constant trim angle.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A planing hull has the following characteristics:*
- *Displacement $W = 150{,}000$ N*
- *Wetted length-beam ratio $\lambda = 4.0$*
- *Deadrise angle $\beta = 15°$*
- *Trim angle $\tau = 4°$*
- *Velocity $V = 25$ m/s*
- *Sea water density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³*

*Compute the lift coefficient using Savitsky's empirical relation:*
$$C_L = \beta^{0.6} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{1 + \lambda}\right)^{0.8} \cdot \tau^{0.6}$$

*Then estimate the mean wetted length required to support the weight.*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

A planing hull operates on the principle of **dynamic lift**. At rest, the hull floats in displacement mode. As forward speed increases, hydrodynamic pressure builds up beneath the flat or V-shaped bottom, generating a vertical lift force. Once this lift equals the weight of the craft, the hull rises out of the water, decreasing wetted surface area and consequently reducing frictional resistance.

**Trim Equilibrium:**

For a planing surface at trim angle $\tau$, two equilibrium conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

**Vertical equilibrium** (lift supports weight):
$$L = W$$

**Horizontal equilibrium** (thrust balances drag):
$$T = R_{total}$$

The hydrodynamic normal force $N$ on the wetted surface has:
- Vertical component: $N\cos\tau = L = W$
- Horizontal component: $N\sin\tau = R$

Dividing the two:
$$\tan\tau = \dfrac{R}{W}$$

This is the **fundamental trim equilibrium equation** for planing craft.

For more precise analysis, the **Savitsky formula** (1964) provides empirical lift coefficient:
$$C_L = \tau^{0.6} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{1+\lambda}\right)^{0.8} \cdot (\sin\beta)^{0.6}$$

(All angles in radians for the original empirical formulation.)

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert angles to radians**
$$\beta = 15° = \dfrac{15\pi}{180} = 0.2618 \text{ rad}$$
$$\tau = 4° = \dfrac{4\pi}{180} = 0.0698 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Savitsky's relation**
$$C_L = (0.2618)^{0.6} \cdot \left(\dfrac{4.0}{1 + 4.0}\right)^{0.8} \cdot (0.0698)^{0.6}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term**
$$(0.2618)^{0.6} = 0.4231$$
$$\left(\dfrac{4}{5}\right)^{0.8} = (0.8)^{0.8} = 0.8365$$
$$(0.0698)^{0.6} = 0.1834$$

**Step 4 — Multiply**
$$C_L = 0.4231 \times 0.8365 \times 0.1834 = 0.0649$$

**Step 5 — Estimate mean wetted length**

The lift relation is:
$$L = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 b^2 C_L$$

Rearranging for wetted beam $b$ (since $L = W$):
$$b = \sqrt{\dfrac{2W}{\rho V^2 C_L}}$$

$$b = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 150{,}000}{1025 \times (25)^2 \times 0.0649}}$$

$$b = \sqrt{\dfrac{300{,}000}{1025 \times 625 \times 0.0649}}$$

$$b = \sqrt{\dfrac{300{,}000}{41{,}590.6}}$$

$$b = \sqrt{7.213} = 2.686 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Mean wetted length**
$$\ell = \lambda \cdot b = 4.0 \times 2.686 = 10.74 \text{ m}$$

**Result:** Mean wetted chine length $\ell \approx 10.74$ m; lift coefficient $C_L \approx 0.0649$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2

**[KTU University Exam — Past Year Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe the working principle of an Air Cushion Vehicle (ACV). Derive the expression for cushion pressure and explain the role of lift fans and skirts.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A hovercraft of weight $W = 80{,}000$ N operates with a rectangular cushion of dimensions $12$ m × $6$ m. The hover gap is $h = 0.3$ m. Assuming circular-equivalent leakage area and air density $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, calculate:*
*(i) The cushion pressure required*
*(ii) The leakage flow rate*
*(iii) The minimum fan power required (assume fan efficiency $\eta_{fan} = 0.7$)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

An Air Cushion Vehicle (ACV or hovercraft) is supported entirely by a **pressurized cushion of air** confined between the hull and the surface (water or land). This eliminates water contact for support, enabling amphibious operation.

**Working Components:**
1. **Lift Fans** — Centrifugal or axial fans that pump air into the cushion plenum.
2. **Cushion Skirt** — A flexible rubber/fabric enclosure that traps the air, allowing the craft to traverse uneven terrain or waves.
3. **Plenum Chamber** — The space beneath the hull where cushion pressure builds up.
4. **Thrust Propellers** — Separate propulsion system (the cushion does not provide forward thrust).

**Cushion Pressure Derivation:**

Force balance on the craft (vertical equilibrium):
$$P_c \cdot A_c = W$$

$$\boxed{P_c = \dfrac{W}{A_c}}$$

Where:
- $P_c$ = cushion gauge pressure [Pa]
- $A_c$ = effective cushion planform area [m²]
- $W$ = craft weight [N]

**Fan Power Requirement:**
To maintain cushion pressure, lift fans must supply volumetric flow $Q$ against leakage $Q_L$:

$$P_{fan} = \dfrac{P_c \cdot Q}{\eta_{fan}}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cushion Area**
$$A_c = 12 \times 6 = 72 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Cushion Pressure (i)**
$$P_c = \dfrac{W}{A_c} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{72} = 1111.11 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent radius (for leakage calculation)**

Using area equivalence for a circle:
$$\pi R^2 = 72 \implies R = \sqrt{\dfrac{72}{\pi}} = \sqrt{22.918} = 4.787 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Leakage velocity (Bernoulli principle)**

Air exits the gap with velocity based on the pressure differential:
$$v_{leak} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 P_c}{\rho_{air}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1111.11}{1.225}}$$

$$v_{leak} = \sqrt{1814.46} = 42.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Leakage Flow Rate (ii)**

For a circular cushion, the leakage perimeter is the circumference:
$$Q_L = 2\pi R \cdot h \cdot v_{leak}$$
$$Q_L = 2\pi \times 4.787 \times 0.3 \times 42.6$$
$$Q_L = 30.08 \times 0.3 \times 42.6$$
$$Q_L = 384.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Fan Power Required (iii)**

$$P_{fan} = \dfrac{P_c \cdot Q_L}{\eta_{fan}} = \dfrac{1111.11 \times 384.4}{0.7}$$

$$P_{fan} = \dfrac{427{,}107}{0.7} = 610{,}153 \text{ W} \approx 610 \text{ kW}$$

**Result Summary:**
- (i) Cushion pressure $P_c = 1111.11$ Pa
- (ii) Leakage flow $Q_L \approx 384.4$ m³/s
- (iii) Fan power $P_{fan} \approx 610.15$ kW

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Angle Conversion:** Students often forget to convert degrees to radians when applying Savitsky's formula. This yields wrong $C_L$ and propagates throughout the problem.
> 2. **Skirt Confusion:** Many students confuse **plenum pressure** (inside cushion) with **ambient pressure** (outside). The formula $P_c = W/A_c$ gives **gauge pressure**.
> 3. **Lift vs Thrust:** ACV lift fans provide vertical support only. Forward propulsion is a **separate system**. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for conflating the two.
> 4. **Froude Number Misuse:** Froude number applies to **hydrodynamic** support only. It is meaningless for ACVs that do not rely on water contact.
> 5. **Savitsky Limitations:** Savitsky's formula assumes **hard chine, prismatic hulls** at moderate speeds. Do not extrapolate to extreme planing conditions without validation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Quick Recall:**

- [x] **Definition:** Unconventional hulls = support other than pure Archimedean buoyancy.
- [x] **Froude Number:** $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ governs wave resistance.
- [x] **Hull Speed Barrier:** $V_{max} \approx 1.34\sqrt{L_{ft}}$ for displacement hulls.
- [x] **Planing Hulls:** Support from dynamic pressure; governed by Savitsky equations.
- [x] **Savitsky Formula:** $C_L = \tau^{0.6}(\lambda/(1+\lambda))^{0.8}(\sin\beta)^{0.6}$ (angles in radians).
- [x] **Hydrofoils:** Lift from circulation; $C_L \approx 2\pi\alpha$ (thin foil).
- [x] **ACV Cushion:** $P_c = W/A_c$; powered by lift fans.
- [x] **SES:** Hybrid — air cushion + sidewall buoyancy.
- [x] **Catamaran:** Reduced wave-making due to slender demi-hulls.
- [x] **SWATH:** Submerged buoyant volumes for stability.
- [x] **Deadrise angle $\beta$:** Controls slamming and spray at high speed.
- [x] **Trim angle $\tau$:** Adjustable via LCG position for performance optimization.
- [x] **Aspect Ratio:** Affects hydrofoil lift and induced drag.
- [x] **Resistance Decomposition:** $R_T = R_F + R_R + R_A + R_B$.
- [x] **ITTC Friction Line:** $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ (turbulent flow on smooth surfaces).
- [x] **Planing vs Displacement Transition:** Occurs around $F_n \approx 0.4$ to $0.5$.
- [x] **Air Cushion Skirt Functions:** Containment, conformity, wave tolerance.

**Memory Aid:** *"Planing flies on water, foils fly in water, hovercraft flies on air, multi-hulls slice the wave."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aero and Hydrodynamic Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST745 - DESIGN OF HIGH SPEED CRAFTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Aero and Hydrodynamic Forces # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What Are Aero and Hydrodynamic Forces? In the context of **Design of High-Speed Crafts**, **Aerodynamic Forces** are the forces generated by the relative motion of the craft through air (or the motion of air over the craft), while **Hydrodynamic Forces** are the forces generated by the relative motion of the hull through water. At high Froude numbers ($F_n > 0.4$), both media interact simultaneously with the craft, producing complex force systems that govern performance, stability, and structural loading. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > *Aerodynamic Forces* refer to the lift, drag, and side-force components created by airflow over the superstructure, windscreen, and any airborne portions of the hull during high-speed operation. *Hydrodynamic Forces* refer to the lift, drag, and pressure components arising from water flow under the hull bottom, planing surfaces, hydrofoils, struts, and appendages. --- ## 1.2 The Two Primary Force Environments | Environment | Medium | Density | Typical Force Type | |---|---|---|---| | Aerodynamic | Air | $\rho_{air} \approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Wind drag, aerodynamic lift on upper hull | | Hydrodynamic | Water (sea) | $\rho_{water} \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Hydrodynamic lift, slamming, frictional drag | > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight** > Because $\rho_{water}$ is roughly **838 times** denser than $\rho_{air}$, hydrodynamic forces dominate the equilibrium of a planing craft — but aerodynamic drag is NOT negligible at speeds above 40 knots, where it can represent **20–35%** of total resistance. --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Stone Skipping on Water Imagine a flat stone you throw across a lake so it **bounces** off the surface repeatedly. The stone skips because of: - The upward **lift** generated by water rushing against its angled underside, - The forward **inertia** you imparted, - The downward **gravity** holding it down, - The **drag** retarding its motion. A **planing high-speed craft** behaves exactly like this skipping stone. The hull bottom is angled (deadrise angle) so that as the boat accelerates, water rushing under the hull produces a **dynamic lift** that lifts most of the hull **out of the water**, dramatically reducing wetted area and hence wave-making resistance. The small remaining underwater surfaces experience intense hydrodynamic pressure, while the now-exposed upper hull experiences significant aerodynamic drag. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Matters in Design** > The designer's job is to **balance** the four forces (hydrodynamic lift, hydrodynamic drag, aerodynamic drag, gravity) so that: > - Wetted area is minimized (efficiency), > - Transverse stability is maintained (safety), > - Slamming loads do not exceed structural limits (integrity), > - Spray generation is acceptable (crew comfort, visibility). --- ## 1.4 The Force Resolution — Body-Fixed Coordinate System All aero and hydrodynamic forces are conventionally resolved along a **right-handed body-fixed axis system**: $$ \begin{aligned} X\text{-axis (longitudinal)} &: \text{Positive forward (bow to stern direction of travel)} \\ Y\text{-axis (transverse)} &: \text{Positive to starboard} \\ Z\text{-axis (vertical)} &: \text{Positive downward (into water)} \end{aligned} $$ The six resulting components are: | Component | Symbol | Direction | |---|---|---| | Surge Force | $X$ | Along $X$ (resistance/thrust) | | Transverse Force (Side Force) | $Y$ | Along $Y$ | | Heave Force (Lift) | $Z$ | Along $Z$ | | Roll Moment | $K$ | About $X$ | | Pitch Moment | $M$ | About $Y$ | | Yaw Moment | $N$ | About $Z$ | > [!NOTE] > For straight-line motion at constant speed and trim, only $X$ (resistance), $Z$ (hydrodynamic lift), and $M$ (trimming moment) are non-zero. This simplification is the **baseline** for almost all KTU ESE Module-2 derivations. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decomposition of Hydrodynamic Force on a Planing Surface Consider a flat planing bottom inclined at small trim angle $\tau$ to the horizontal, moving at velocity $V$ through still water. The wetted length is $\lambda \cdot L$ where $\lambda$ is the **wetted-length ratio** (typically 0.6–0.85 for fully planing craft) and $L$ is the planing length (chord). ### Force Components (per unit span) The integrated hydrodynamic pressure along the wetted bottom produces two orthogonal force components in the **flow-aligned frame**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Lift (vertical)} &: L_h = \int_0^{\lambda L} p(x)\cos\tau \, dx \\ \text{Drag (horizontal)} &: D_h = \int_0^{\lambda L} p(x)\sin\tau \, dx \end{aligned} $$ Where $p(x)$ is the local hydrodynamic pressure beneath the planing surface. --- ## 2.2 The Dynamic Pressure Foundation The fundamental starting point for **every** aero and hydrodynamic force in high-speed craft is the **dynamic pressure**: $$ q = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V^2 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept — Dynamic Pressure** > - For air at 15°C: $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - For sea water at 15°C: $\rho_{sea} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Total pressure at stagnation point: $p_0 = p_{atm} + q$ > - **Lifting surfaces** of high-speed craft operate in the **inviscid pressure-dominated** regime (pressure recovery is the source of lift), while **slender appendages** are typically **viscous-dominated** (skin friction is the main contributor). --- ## 2.3 Hydrodynamic Force Coefficients Just like in aerodynamics, hydrodynamic forces are non-dimensionalised using **force coefficients** based on a reference area $A_{ref}$, the relative velocity $V$, and the fluid density $\rho$: $$ C_L = \frac{L}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_{ref}}, \qquad C_D = \frac{D}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_{ref}}, \qquad C_Z = \frac{Z}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A_{ref}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Reference Area Choice (varies with craft type)** > - **Planing hulls**: $A_{ref} = b^2$ (beam squared — Savitsky convention) > - **Hydrofoils**: $A_{ref} = $ foil planform area ($c \cdot b$) > - **Airborne parts (aerodynamic)**: $A_{ref} = $ frontal projected area > - **Round bilge displacement hulls**: $A_{ref} = \nabla^{2/3}$ (ITTC-78) --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / When to Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ | Dynamic pressure — foundational to ALL force equations | Pa | | 2 | $L = C_L \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A$ | General lift equation (aero or hydro) | N | | 3 | $D = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A$ | General drag equation (aero or hydro) | N | | 4 | $R_n = \frac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number — viscous scaling | dimensionless | | 5 | $F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude number — gravity/wave scaling | dimensionless | | 6 | $M = \frac{V}{a}$ | Mach number — compressibility onset (M > 0.3 matters) | dimensionless | | 7 | $p_{slam} = \rho c V$ | **Hugon slamming pressure** (impact) | Pa | | 8 | $L = C_L \rho V^2 b^2$ | Savitsky lift for **planing hulls** | N | | 9 | $C_{L_\beta} = \tau + 0.0005 \cdot (LCG/L)^2 \cdot (V/\sqrt{gL})$ | Savitsky lift coefficient formula | dimensionless | | 10 | $C_{D} = C_{L_\beta} \cdot \tan\tau + C_F$ | Savitsky drag coefficient | dimensionless | | 11 | $C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | ITTC-78 frictional line (for FRICTION only) | dimensionless | | 12 | $L_{foil} = \rho V^2 b c \cdot C_{L_{foil}}$ | Hydrofoil lift per unit wing | N | | 13 | $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{air} V_{rel}^2 A_f C_{D,aero}$ | Aerodynamic drag on superstructure | N | | 14 | $V_{rel} = V_{craft} \pm V_{wind}$ | Relative velocity between craft and air | m/s | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap** > When applying Hugon's slamming pressure, the speed $V$ is the **vertical impact velocity** ($V_z = V \sin\tau$), NOT the craft's forward speed. Many students lose 2–3 marks by using the wrong velocity component. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why These Forces Matter | |---|---| | **Planing hull design** (fast patrol boats, racing yachts) | Lift–weight balance determines trim angle and wetted area → speed vs. power | | **Hydrofoil craft** (passenger ferries) | Foil lift must support full displacement weight with small wetted area | | **SES / Air-cushion vehicles** | Combined aerodynamic + cushion pressure lifts the hull clear of water | | **Structural scantling (slamming)** | Peak pressures dictate plate thickness at keel, chines, and bow | | **Wind tunnel / CFD validation** | Force coefficients allow scale-model extrapolation to full-scale | --- ## 2.6 Why These Equations Work — The Physical "Why" | Step | What Happens | Why It Generates Force | |---|---|---| | 1 | Craft moves at velocity $V$ | Water/air has relative velocity $-V$ | | 2 | Fluid is deflected by the hull/foil | Momentum change → reaction force | | 3 | Curved/angled surface accelerates fluid | Bernoulli's principle: $p \downarrow \Rightarrow F \uparrow$ | | 4 | Pressure integral over wetted surface | Net force = vector sum of $p \, dA$ over surface | | 5 | Non-dimensionalise by $q \cdot A$ | Obtain $C_L$, $C_D$ independent of $V$, $\rho$ | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate** > Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly. No skipped lines, no shortcuts. Each derivation below is the **full examiner-grade working** expected in a KTU ESE answer script. --- ## 3.1 Derivation I — Dynamic Pressure from Euler's Equation We start with the **steady, inviscid, incompressible Euler equation** along a streamline: $$ \frac{dp}{\rho} + V\,dV = 0 \quad \text{(Bernoulli along streamline)} $$ **Step 1.** Integrate from a far-field point (where pressure is $p_\infty$ and velocity is $V_\infty$) to a stagnation point on the hull (where pressure is $p_0$ and velocity is 0): $$ \int_{p_\infty}^{p_0} \frac{dp}{\rho} + \int_{V_\infty}^{0} V\,dV = 0 $$ **Step 2.** Assuming constant density $\rho$ (incompressible flow — valid for $M < 0.3$): $$ \frac{p_0 - p_\infty}{\rho} + \left[\frac{V^2}{2}\right]_{V_\infty}^{0} = 0 $$ **Step 3.** Evaluate the velocity integral: $$ \frac{p_0 - p_\infty}{\rho} - \frac{V_\infty^2}{2} = 0 $$ **Step 4.** Solve for the pressure difference: $$ p_0 - p_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 $$ **Step 5.** Define this pressure difference as the **dynamic pressure** $q$: $$ q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \quad \blacksquare $$ > This is the **maximum pressure** that can be generated on any surface moving at speed $V$ through an incompressible fluid. It is the universal scaling variable for both aerodynamic and hydrodynamic forces. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Hydrodynamic Lift on a 2-D Flat Plate at Small Trim (Savitsky's Foundation) Consider an **infinite-span flat plate** of chord $L$, inclined at a small trim angle $\tau$ to the free stream of velocity $V$. The wetted length is $\lambda L$, where $0 < \lambda \le 1$. **Step 1.** Choose a coordinate $x$ measured from the leading edge of the wetted region. The local plate velocity component **normal to the plate** is $V_n = V\sin\tau \approx V\tau$ (small-angle). **Step 2.** From thin-plate theory (Wagner, 1929; Savitsky, 1964), the pressure coefficient on the wetted surface in the **2-D strip** is approximately: $$ C_p(x) = \frac{p(x)}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2} \approx \frac{2\tau}{\sqrt{1 - (x/\lambda L)^2}} $$ **Step 3.** The **lift per unit span** is the normal pressure integrated over the wetted length, then projected vertically: $$ \frac{dL}{db} = \int_0^{\lambda L} p(x)\cos\tau\,dx \approx \cos\tau\int_0^{\lambda L} \rho V^2 \tau \cdot \frac{dx}{\sqrt{1 - (x/\lambda L)^2}} $$ **Step 4.** Substitute the trig identity $\cos\tau \approx 1$ (small angle) and factor: $$ \frac{dL}{db} = \rho V^2 \tau \int_0^{\lambda L}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{1 - (x/\lambda L)^2}} $$ **Step 5.** Apply the standard integral substitution $u = x/(\lambda L)$: $$ \int_0^{\lambda L}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{1 - (x/\lambda L)^2}} = \lambda L\int_0^1 \frac{du}{\sqrt{1-u^2}} = \lambda L \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} $$ **Step 6.** Therefore: $$ \frac{dL}{db} = \frac{\pi}{2}\,\rho V^2 \tau \lambda L $$ **Step 7.** Total lift on a finite-span plate (multiply by beam $b$): $$ L = \frac{\pi}{2}\,\rho V^2 \tau \lambda L b $$ **Step 8.** Savitsky's empirical fit adjusts the $\pi/2$ factor and introduces an empirical correction. He defined the **lift coefficient based on $b^2$**: $$ C_{L_\beta} = \frac{L}{\rho V^2 b^2} $$ Solving the above for $C_{L_\beta}$ and rearranging to incorporate the empirical **LCG correction** (load center position from transom), Savitsky's working formula becomes: $$ C_{L_\beta} = \tau + 0.0005 \cdot \left(\frac{LCG}{L}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} \quad \blacksquare $$ > This equation is the **workhorse** of planing-hull resistance prediction. The $0.0005$ empirical constant was fitted by Savitsky from a wide range of prismatic planing hull model tests. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Savitsky Drag Coefficient for Planing Hulls **Step 1.** Total drag on a planing hull is the sum of: - **Pressure-induced drag** $D_p$ (from the same pressure field that produces lift), and - **Frictional drag** $D_f$ (from the boundary layer on the wetted bottom). **Step 2.** The pressure drag per unit span, by analogous derivation to lift: $$ \frac{dD_p}{db} = \tau \cdot \frac{dL}{db}\cdot\frac{1}{1} \approx \tau \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}\rho V^2 \tau \lambda L $$ **Step 3.** For small trim angles, $\tan\tau \approx \tau$, so: $$ D_p \approx L \cdot \tan\tau \approx L\tau $$ **Step 4.** Non-dimensionalising using $C_{L_\beta}$: $$ C_{D_p} = C_{L_\beta}\cdot \tan\tau $$ **Step 5.** Adding the **ITTC-78 frictional coefficient** (skin friction on the wetted bottom): $$ C_{D} = C_{L_\beta}\cdot \tan\tau + C_F $$ **Step 6.** With $C_F$ from ITTC-78: $$ C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}R_n - 2)^2} \quad \blacksquare $$ > For a typical planing craft at $R_n = 10^8$: $C_F \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$. For $C_{L_\beta} = 0.6$ and $\tau = 4°$ ($0.0698$ rad), $C_{D_p} \approx 0.042$. The total $C_D \approx 0.044$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Hugon's Slamming Pressure Slamming is the impulsive pressure when a portion of the hull bottom impacts the water surface, typically at the bow during wave negotiation. **Step 1.** Hugon (1924) proposed that the peak pressure during impact is proportional to: - the local water density $\rho$, - the local wave-propagation speed $c = \sqrt{g\,d}$ in shallow water (or $\sqrt{g\lambda_w}$ in deep water), and - the normal impact velocity $V_n$. **Step 2.** Hugon's empirical relation: $$ p_{slam} = \rho \, c \, V_n $$ **Step 3.** For a planing craft in deep water, the characteristic celerity is $c = \sqrt{g\lambda_w}$, where $\lambda_w$ is the dominant wavelength. Using $V_n = V\sin\tau \approx V\tau$: $$ p_{slam} \approx \rho V\tau \sqrt{g\lambda_w} $$ **Step 4.** Dimensionally: $[\rho][V][\tau][\sqrt{g\lambda_w}] = \frac{kg}{m^3} \cdot \frac{m}{s} \cdot \text{rad} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{m}{s^2}\cdot m} = \frac{kg}{m\cdot s^2} = \text{Pa}$ ✓ **Step 5.** For a 40-knot craft ($V \approx 20.6$ m/s) with $\tau = 4°$ ($\approx 0.0698$ rad) and $\lambda_w = 30$ m: $$ p_{slam} \approx 1025 \times 20.6 \times 0.0698 \times \sqrt{9.81\times 30} $$ $$ p_{slam} \approx 1473 \times \sqrt{294.3} \approx 1473 \times 17.16 \approx 25{,}275 \text{ Pa} \approx 25.3 \text{ kPa} $$ This is approximately **2.5 tonnes per square metre** — a structural load that absolutely cannot be ignored. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation V — Aerodynamic Drag on Exposed Superstructure **Step 1.** The relative wind speed seen by the craft's superstructure is: $$ V_{rel} = V_{craft} - V_{headwind} $$ **Step 2.** Aerodynamic drag force: $$ F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_{air} V_{rel}^2 \, A_f \, C_{D,aero} $$ Where $A_f$ is the **frontal projected area** of the superstructure (windscreen, mast, cabin), and $C_{D,aero}$ depends on geometry. **Step 3.** Typical values: | Superstructure Shape | $C_{D,aero}$ | |---|---| | Streamlined cabin | 0.20 – 0.35 | | Boxy wheelhouse | 0.60 – 0.80 | | Mast + rigging | 1.00 – 1.20 | | Windscreen only (sloped) | 0.15 – 0.25 | **Step 4.** At $V = 40$ knots ($20.6$ m/s), $A_f = 4$ m², $C_{D,aero} = 0.5$: $$ F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (20.6)^2 \times 4 \times 0.5 \approx 519 \text{ N} $$ **Step 5.** Compare with hydrodynamic drag of same craft (~6 kN): aerodynamic component is ~9%, confirming it is **non-negligible** but **secondary**. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.6 KTU Examiner's Worked-Example Matrix | # | Problem Type | Key Formula Used | Expected Steps | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Calculate hydrodynamic lift given $V, \tau, b, L$ | $C_{L_\beta}$ (Savitsky) | (1) $V/\sqrt{gL}$, (2) compute $C_{L_\beta}$, (3) $L = C_{L_\beta}\rho V^2 b^2$ | | 2 | Compute planing craft drag | Savitsky drag eqn. | (1) $C_{L_\beta}$, (2) $C_D$, (3) $D = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 b^2$ | | 3 | Compute slamming pressure | Hugon's formula | (1) Find $V_n = V\sin\tau$, (2) Find $c$, (3) Multiply | | 4 | Aerodynamic drag on cabin | $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_{air} V^2 A_f C_D$ | Direct substitution | | 5 | Reynolds-number scaling for friction | $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ | (1) compute $R_n$, (2) plug into ITTC | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Force System on a Planing Craft ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Craft Motion State
Velocity V, Trim Angle tau] --> B[Incoming Water Flow
rho_water, V] A --> C[Incoming Air Flow
rho_air, V_rel] B --> D[Hull Bottom
Wetted Region] B --> E[Struts and Foils
if fitted] B --> F[Transom and Spray Sheet] C --> G[Superstructure
Cabin, Mast] C --> H[Upper Exposed Hull
above waterline] D --> D1[Dynamic Pressure
q = 1/2 rho V^2] D1 --> D2[Pressure Distribution
p x on wetted bottom] D2 --> D3[Lift Force L_h
vertical, upward] D2 --> D4[Drag Force D_h
longitudinal, aft] D3 --> X[Force Equilibrium Solver
Six-component balance] D4 --> X E --> E1[Foil Lift L_foil
vertical, supports weight] E --> E2[Foil Drag D_foil
longitudinal, aft] E1 --> X E2 --> X F --> F1[Spray Drag D_spray
energy loss to spray] F1 --> X G --> G1[Aerodynamic Drag D_aero
frontal pressure + skin friction] H --> H1[Wind-Induced Side Force
if beam wind present] G1 --> X H1 --> X X --> Y[Net Resistance R_total
= D_h + D_foil + D_spray + D_aero] X --> Z[Net Lift L_total
= L_h + L_foil + Aerodynamic Lift] Y --> OUT1[Required Thrust T
= R_total at constant V] Z --> OUT2[Heave Equilibrium Check
L_total = W = m*g] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Cause-and-Effect Map of Hydrodynamic Lift Generation ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1
Craft Accelerates
V increases] --> P2[Step 2
Dynamic Pressure Rises
q = 1/2 rho V^2] P2 --> P3[Step 3
Bow Rises
Trim Angle Increases] P3 --> P4[Step 4
Wetted Area Shrinks
lambda L decreases] P4 --> P5[Step 5
Lift Coefficient Climbs
CL_beta grows] P5 --> P6[Step 6
Lift Matches Weight
Steady Planing] P6 --> P7{Step 7
Equilibrium?
Check L = W} P7 -->|Yes| P8[Constant-Velocity Planing
Stable Operating Point] P7 -->|No| P9[Adjust Trim or Throttle
to restore equilibrium] P9 --> P1 ``` ## 4.3 Free-Body Force Resolution Diagram (Schematic Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TD HULL[Planing Hull at Trim tau] W[Weight W = m*g
acting downward] --> HULL LIFT[Hydrodynamic Lift L_h
acting upward] --> HULL RDRAG[Resistance R_total
acting aftward] --> HULL THRUST[Engine Thrust T
acting forward] --> HULL MDISP[Weight Disp.
near amidships] --> HULL MLIFT[Hull LCG of Lift
near transom] --> HULL MDISP -.generates.-> PM[Pitching Moment M_pitch] MLIFT -.generates.-> PM PM --> TRIM[Trim Angle tau stabilises
when M_pitch = 0] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note** > The Mermaid diagrams above are **block-functional architecture** representations. The transverse stability (roll), the yaw coupling (drift), and the lateral hydrodynamic side force (in turns) follow the same architecture but with different reference axes. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the term "dynamic pressure" as applied to high-speed craft hydrodynamic analysis. Why is it the natural scaling variable for hydrodynamic lift and drag?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Dynamic pressure is the kinetic energy per unit volume of a moving fluid, defined as $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ **[1 mark]**, where $\rho$ is fluid density and $V$ is the relative velocity between the craft and fluid. It represents the maximum pressure rise achievable on a stagnation surface **[1 mark]**. Because both lift and drag on streamlined bodies in inviscid flow scale linearly with $q$, it is the natural non-dimensionalising quantity, producing velocity- and density-independent force coefficients $C_L$ and $C_D$ **[1 mark]**. --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State Hugon's formula for slamming pressure. What is the correct velocity to use in this formula?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Hugon's formula for slamming pressure is $p_{slam} = \rho \, c \, V_n$ **[1 mark]**, where $\rho$ is water density, $c$ is the local wave celerity ($c = \sqrt{g\,d}$ or $c = \sqrt{g\lambda_w}$), and $V_n$ is the **normal impact velocity component** **[1 mark]**, given by $V_n = V\sin\tau \approx V\tau$ for small trim angles. The correct velocity is the **component normal to the water surface**, not the forward speed of the craft **[1 mark]**. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule**: Internal choice between two questions. Each sub-question is 7 marks. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set B]` **(a)** Derive, starting from the Bernoulli equation, an expression for the dynamic pressure on a stagnation point of a body moving at velocity $V$ through an incompressible fluid of density $\rho$. Hence, write the general expressions for hydrodynamic lift and drag on a submerged planing surface. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Bernoulli's Equation along a streamtube:** $$ \frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{V^2}{2} = \text{constant} $$ **Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions at infinity ($p_\infty, V_\infty$) and at stagnation ($p_0, V=0$):** $$ \frac{p_\infty}{\rho} + \frac{V_\infty^2}{2} = \frac{p_0}{\rho} + 0 $$ **Step 3 — Solve for stagnation pressure rise:** $$ p_0 - p_\infty = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 $$ **Step 4 — Define dynamic pressure:** $$ q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \quad \text{[2 marks]} $$ **Step 5 — General lift expression:** $$ L = C_L \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \cdot A_{ref} = C_L \cdot q \cdot A_{ref} \quad \text{[1.5 marks]} $$ **Step 6 — General drag expression:** $$ D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 \cdot A_{ref} = C_D \cdot q \cdot A_{ref} \quad \text{[1.5 marks]} $$ **Step 7 — Comment on the choice of $A_{ref}$ for planing hulls vs. hydrofoils:** For planing hulls $A_{ref} = b^2$ (beam squared, Savitsky convention); for hydrofoils $A_{ref} = $ planform area $b \times c$. **[1 mark for the closing remark]** --- **(b)** A planing craft has the following particulars: - Length (chord) $L = 8$ m - Beam $b = 2.6$ m - Mass $m = 4000$ kg - Forward speed $V = 18$ m/s ($\approx 35$ knots) - Wetted-length ratio $\lambda = 0.75$ - Trim angle $\tau = 4°$ Compute the hydrodynamic lift using Savitsky's formula and verify whether it supports the craft's weight. (Use $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.) **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the velocity-length Froude number:** $$ \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} = \frac{18}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 8}} = \frac{18}{\sqrt{78.48}} = \frac{18}{8.86} = 2.032 \quad \text{[1 mark]} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Savitsky's lift coefficient formula** (assuming $LCG/L$ position contributes negligibly, or use $LCG = -2\%$ for typical): $$ C_{L_\beta} = \tau + 0.0005 \cdot \left(\frac{LCG}{L}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} $$ Assuming $LCG = 0$ (transom-centred lift): $C_{L_\beta} = \tau = 4° = 0.0698$ rad **[1 mark]**. **Step 3 — Compute the lift force:** $$ L = C_{L_\beta} \cdot \rho V^2 b^2 = 0.0698 \times 1025 \times (18)^2 \times (2.6)^2 $$ $$ L = 0.0698 \times 1025 \times 324 \times 6.76 $$ $$ L = 0.0698 \times 2{,}245{,}218 = 156{,}716 \text{ N} \approx 156.7 \text{ kN} \quad \text{[2 marks]} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the craft's weight:** $$ W = mg = 4000 \times 9.81 = 39{,}240 \text{ N} \approx 39.24 \text{ kN} \quad \text{[1 mark]} $$ **Step 5 — Compare:** $$ L = 156.7 \text{ kN} \quad \gg \quad W = 39.24 \text{ kN} $$ Lift is **4× the weight** — the craft is well above planing condition. **[1 mark]** **Step 6 — Concluding comment:** Either the trim must reduce (lower angle) or the craft is in an over-trimmed transient. In practice the wetted-length ratio $\lambda$ would decrease, and the equilibrium trim would settle at a smaller angle. **[1 mark]** --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the aid of a sketch, describe the various components of aerodynamic and hydrodynamic forces acting on a high-speed planing craft at constant trim. Identify the dominant force and explain why it dominates. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Forces acting on a planing craft at constant trim include:** | Force | Medium | Approximate Magnitude | Direction | |---|---|---|---| | Hydrodynamic lift $L_h$ | Water | ~equal to weight $W$ | Vertical (up) | | Hydrodynamic drag $D_h$ (pressure + frictional) | Water | 60–80% of total resistance | Aft | | Spray drag $D_{spray}$ | Water/air interface | 5–15% of total resistance | Aft | | Aerodynamic drag $D_{aero}$ | Air | 5–20% of total resistance | Aft | | Weight $W = mg$ | — | By definition | Vertical (down) | | Thrust $T$ | Propeller/waterjet | Equal to total $R$ at constant $V$ | Forward | **[3 marks for the comprehensive table]** **Sketch description** (since a physical sketch is required in script): ``` D_aero ↑ | +----------+----------+ | Hull | T → | ========= | ← D_h, D_spray | (planing) | +----=====-----======+ ↑ ↓ L_h W ``` **[2 marks for sketch]** **Dominant Force — Hydrodynamic Lift:** At high speed, $L_h$ is the dominant force because it alone must support the full weight of the craft while the wetted area is small. Without sufficient $L_h$, the craft cannot rise onto the plane and the benefits of planing (low wave-making resistance) are lost. The hydrodynamic lift scales with $\rho V^2$, and since $\rho_{water}/\rho_{air} \approx 838$, even small immersed surfaces produce forces orders of magnitude larger than aerodynamic ones on equal areas. **[2 marks]** --- **(b)** A 50-knot patrol boat has frontal superstructure area $A_f = 6$ m² and aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_{D,aero} = 0.6$. The craft is running into a 10-knot headwind. Compute the aerodynamic drag force and express it as a percentage of the craft's total resistance, which is $R_{total} = 22$ kN. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert speeds to m/s:** $$ V_{craft} = 50 \text{ knots} = 50 \times 0.5144 = 25.72 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{[1 mark]} $$ $$ V_{headwind} = 10 \text{ knots} = 10 \times 0.5144 = 5.144 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute relative wind speed:** $$ V_{rel} = V_{craft} + V_{headwind} = 25.72 + 5.144 = 30.86 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{[1 mark]} $$ **Step 3 — Apply aerodynamic drag equation:** $$ F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_{air} V_{rel}^2 \, A_f \, C_{D,aero} $$ $$ F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (30.86)^2 \times 6 \times 0.6 $$ $$ F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 952.34 \times 3.6 $$ $$ F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 3428.42 = 2099.9 \text{ N} \approx 2.10 \text{ kN} \quad \text{[3 marks]} $$ **Step 4 — Compute percentage of total resistance:** $$ \% = \frac{F_{aero}}{R_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{2.10}{22} \times 100 = 9.55\% \quad \text{[1 mark]} $$ **Step 5 — Concluding comment:** Aerodynamic drag accounts for **~9.5%** of total resistance — a non-negligible contribution that justifies streamlined cabin design in modern fast patrol craft. In a **following wind** of 10 knots, the relative speed would drop to ~20.6 m/s and aerodynamic drag would fall to ~0.94 kN (~4.3%), illustrating the strong sensitivity of aerodynamic drag to wind direction. **[1 mark]** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Aero and Hydrodynamic Force Problems** > > 1. **Using forward velocity $V$ instead of normal component $V\sin\tau$ in slamming formula** → loses 1–2 marks immediately. *Always use $V_n = V\sin\tau$ for vertical impact speed.* > > 2. **Wrong reference area $A_{ref}$** → many students use planform area for planing hulls; the Savitsky convention is **$b^2$** (beam squared). For hydrofoils, use $b \cdot c$. This single error propagates through 3–4 sub-steps and costs 3 marks. > > 3. **Forgetting to convert trim angle from degrees to radians** in the Savitsky lift formula $C_{L_\beta} = \tau + \ldots$. Without the conversion, the $0.0005$ empirical term and the $\tau$ term are inconsistent in units. > > 4. **Mixing air and water densities** → when computing aerodynamic drag, $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³ must be used; for hydrodynamic, $\rho_{water} = 1025$ kg/m³. Students often use 1000 kg/m³ (fresh water) for sea-water craft problems, losing the 2.5% accuracy. > > 5. **ITTC-78 formula uses $\log_{10}$, not $\ln$** → $C_F = 0.075/(\log_{10}R_n - 2)^2$. Using natural log gives an answer off by a factor of 2.3, costing all downstream marks. > > 6. **Assuming $L_h = W$ is the equilibrium condition** — this is true **only** at constant speed and constant trim. During acceleration, $L_h - W \neq 0$, and the craft is in heave acceleration. Examiners expect students to **state** the equilibrium precondition. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) ### Core Concepts - [x] **Dynamic pressure** $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ — universal scaling variable. - [x] **Hydrodynamic lift** dominates high-speed equilibrium (water is ~838× denser than air). - [x] **Aerodynamic drag** is non-negligible above ~30 knots; superstructure streamlining is essential. - [x] **Planing hull** generates dynamic lift on the angled bottom — analogous to a skipping stone. - [x] **Hydrofoil** generates lift on a fully submerged wing — supports entire craft weight. ### Key Equations (Must Memorise) - [x] General lift: $L = C_L \cdot q \cdot A$ - [x] General drag: $D = C_D \cdot q \cdot A$ - [x] Savitsky lift coefficient: $C_{L_\beta} = \tau + 0.0005 (LCG/L)^2 (V/\sqrt{gL})$ - [x] Savitsky drag: $C_D = C_{L_\beta}\tan\tau + C_F$ - [x] ITTC-78 friction: $C_F = 0.075/(\log R_n - 2)^2$ - [x] Hugon slamming: $p_{slam} = \rho c V_n$ - [x] Reynolds number: $R_n = VL/\nu$ - [x] Froude number: $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ ### Reference Areas - [x] Planing hulls: $A_{ref} = b^2$ - [x] Hydrofoils: $A_{ref} = b \cdot c$ - [x] Aerodynamic (superstructure): $A_{ref} = A_{frontal}$ - [x] Displacement hulls: $A_{ref} = \nabla^{2/3}$ ### Standard Densities (At 15°C) - [x] Sea water: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ - [x] Air: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ - [x] Standard gravity: $g = 9.81$ m/s² ### Conversion - [x] 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s - [x] 1° = $\pi/180$ rad $\approx$ 0.01745 rad ### Common Pitfalls (Avoid!) - [x] Don't use $V$ instead of $V\sin\tau$ for slamming. - [x] Don't use planform area for Savitsky — use $b^2$. - [x] Don't use $\ln$ for ITTC-78 — use $\log_{10}$. - [x] Don't assume $L_h = W$ outside steady-state. - [x] Don't forget the relative-wind correction for aerodynamic drag. ### Conceptual Anchors - [x] **Bernoulli → Dynamic pressure** is the cornerstone. - [x] **Force coefficients** are non-dimensionalised by $q \cdot A_{ref}$. - [x] **Pressure integrates to force**; shear integrates to friction drag. - [x] **Lift = weight** is the steady planing equilibrium condition.

topics.title: Resistance of Very Slender Hulls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST745 - DESIGN OF HIGH SPEED CRAFTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance of Very Slender Hulls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of high-speed craft hydrodynamics, a **Very Slender Hull** is formally defined as a displacement-type marine vessel whose principal geometric proportions yield a **Slenderness Ratio** sufficiently large that classical 3D potential flow theory can be mathematically decomposed into tractable 2D stripwise or slender-body sectional solutions. The KTU 2024 Scheme adopts the volumetric slenderness ratio as the primary classifier:

$$\overline{\nabla}^{1/3} / L \;\;\text{or equivalently}\;\; L / \nabla^{1/3}$$

A hull is classified as **"Very Slender"** when the slenderness ratio $L / \nabla^{1/3} \geq 6.0$ (approximately). For such forms, wave-making resistance can be predicted using **Michell's Thin-Ship Theory (1898)** and its modern extensions such as **Hughe's Slender-Body Method**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A "very slender hull" is one where the half-beam to length ratio $\frac{B}{2L} < 0.10$, permitting the use of linearised thin-ship theory. Slenderness permits neglect of lateral cross-coupling in the wave integral, enabling decoupling of interference components.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pencil in Water" Intuition

Imagine pushing a **sharpened pencil** (slender, long, pointy) vertically through the surface of a calm pond. The water barely "feels" it coming — only a thin, low wake forms. Now push a **short, fat log** (blunt, stubby) through the same pond. A massive, divergent wave system explodes outward. Both displace the same volume, but the geometry completely changes the wave system.

**The slender hull is the "pencil."** Because its beam is so small relative to its length, each transverse section "sees" the flow almost as if it were in isolation (2D flow). The wave system is therefore a *linear superposition* of 2D wave contributions along the length — this is the physical basis of **slender body theory**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Board Exams:** The examiner rewards students who can state *why* slender theory works physically (cross-flow decoupling), not just quote formulas. Marks are routinely awarded for a one-line physical insight before the math begins.

### 1.3 Classification of Slender Craft by $L/\nabla^{1/3}$

| Craft Type | $L/\nabla^{1/3}$ | Theoretical Method Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional ship** | 4.0 – 6.0 | Empirical + 3D Panel (Rankine) |
| **Slender hull** | 6.0 – 8.0 | Slender body / Strip theory |
| **Very slender hull** | $\geq$ 8.0 | Michell / Hughes slender body |
| **Ultra-slender (SES, hydrofoil strut)** | $\geq$ 10.0 | Lifting-line + 2D sections |

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Seawater density:** $\rho = 1025 \;\text{kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ at 15 °C
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$
- **Reference area for resistance coefficient:** $S = \nabla^{2/3}$ (volumetric) or wetted surface $S_W$
- **Speed metric:** **Froude Number** $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$
- **Length metric:** $L_{WL}$ (waterline length), typically used in $F_n$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Total Resistance (Newton's Framework)

For any displacement hull operating at the free surface, the **total calm-water resistance** $R_T$ is decomposed per the **1878 ITTC Standard** (still foundational):

$$R_T = R_F \;+\; R_R \;+\; R_W \;+\; R_{APP}$$

For slender hulls the air resistance $R_{APP}$ is negligible, and the residual resistance $R_R$ absorbs all wave and form effects.

### 2.2 The Slender-Body Decomposition (Hughes, 1956)

For **very slender hulls**, the wave-making resistance is *additively* split into three physically distinct components, each computed from a different mathematical model:

$$R_W = R_{T} \;+\; R_{Th} \;+\; R_{L}$$

where:

- $R_{T}$ = **Thin-ship wave resistance** — from the projection of the hull's waterplane area onto the longitudinal plane (Michell's integral).
- $R_{Th}$ = **Thickness (buoyancy) resistance** — from the finite vertical extent of the hull cross-section, related to the lateral $a(y,z)$ distribution.
- $R_{L}$ = **Lifting (interference) resistance** — from asymmetric flow around a non-wall-sided section, modelled as a 2D hydrofoil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three terms?** Because slender theory *linearises* the boundary condition on the hull. Each physical effect (longitudinal wave generation, vertical thickness, and asymmetric lift) satisfies the linearised free-surface condition independently. This is a HUGELY important exam point — examiners test whether students understand that **the splitting is a physical/linearisation consequence**, not an arbitrary bookkeeping trick.

### 2.3 The Form Factor Method (Prohaska, 1948)

For the **viscous part**, the slender-hull resistance estimate uses the **Prohaska form factor** $(1+k)$ applied to the ITTC friction line:

$$R_V = (1+k)\,R_F$$

The $k$ factor is experimentally determined from a model in a towing tank (the "Prohaska plot" intercept) but for slender hulls a reasonable engineering estimate is:

$$k \;\approx\; 0.15 \;-\; 0.25 \quad \text{(typical slender hull range)}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Froude Number | $F_n = V/\sqrt{gL}$ | $V$ [m/s], $L$ [m] |
| 2 | Slenderness Ratio | $S_R = L / \nabla^{1/3}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | ITTC Friction (1978) | $C_F = \dfrac{0.075}{(\log R_n - 2)^2}$ | $R_n = VL/\nu$ |
| 4 | Frictional Resistance | $R_F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S \, C_F$ | $S$ = wetted surface [m²] |
| 5 | Viscous Resistance | $R_V = (1+k)\,R_F$ | Prohaska form factor |
| 6 | Michell's Wave Resist. | $R_W = \dfrac{\rho g}{2\pi}\!\int\!\!\int P(\theta)\,P(\theta_1)\,\cos^2\!\theta\,\cdot\,\dfrac{e^{-k^2(\alpha^2+\beta^2)}\,\sin(k(\beta-\beta_1))}{k^2}\,dk\,dl\,dl_1$ | See §3.2 |
| 7 | Wave Number | $k = g/V^2$ | $k$ in 1/m at $F_n$ |
| 8 | Interference Coefficient | $\alpha_R = (1+k)\,R_F/R_T - 1$ | KTU standard |
| 9 | Total Resistance | $R_T = (1+k)R_F + R_W$ | Slender hull model |
| 10 | Effective Horsepower | $EHP = R_T \cdot V / 75$ | In metric HP (KTU convention) |
| 11 | Residuary-Resistance Coefficient | $C_R = R_R / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 S)$ | For series comparison |
| 12 | Lifting Resistance (2D foil) | $R_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 c \, C_{L\alpha}\,\alpha^2$ | Per-section, strip-summed |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always quote the **friction line and form factor explicitly**. Examiners deduct marks when students write "frictional resistance" without naming **ITTC 1978** and Prohaska's $(1+k)$ method.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

1. **Concept design of planing hulls and SWATHs:** Slender-body theory offers first-cut resistance before committing to expensive model towing.
2. **Systematic series extrapolation (Holtrop, 1984):** Slender forms are benchmarked against the **Series 64** and **Duisburg** test data sets using $L/\nabla^{1/3}$.
3. **Acoustic signature estimation:** Wave interference is the primary source of hull-pressure fluctuation (RANS validation target).
4. **Acoustic submarine signature (periscope-depth regime):** Slender body is the operational envelope.
