topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST745 - DESIGN OF HIGH SPEED CRAFTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Powering and Propulsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚓ MODULE 3: POWERING AND PROPULSION OF HIGH-SPEED CRAFTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In the context of KTU's **PENST745 (Design of High Speed Crafts)**, **Powering** is defined as the total installed shaft power (or total propulsive power input) required to overcome the resistance of a high-speed vessel and achieve its design speed in a given sea condition, accounting for propulsive efficiency, mechanical transmission losses, and a **Service Margin (SM)**. **Propulsion**, on the other hand, is the engineering discipline concerned with the conversion of this mechanical (or electrical) power into useful thrust that drives the craft forward, with a focus on devices optimized for the unique hydrodynamic regime of high-speed marine vehicles (Froude Number $F_n > 0.5$).

For KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is mapped to the following Course Outcomes (CO):
- **CO3**: Estimate the powering requirements of various high-speed craft types and recommend suitable propulsion systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> For high-speed craft, classical displacement-hull powering methods (Holtrop-Mennen, Series 60) lose accuracy beyond $F_n \approx 0.45$. The KTU curriculum therefore mandates the use of **statistical/parametric powering models** (Savitsky, Mercier & Savitsky, Hoerner, Clement) and emphasizes the selection of **non-conventional propulsors** such as waterjets, surface-piercing propellers, and supercavitating propellers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **cycling on flat ground versus cycling down a steep hill with a parachute tied to your back**. On flat ground, your legs (the **engine**) easily overcome air drag and rolling resistance (analogous to **frictional resistance**). Down the hill with a parachute, the *drag* is enormous — the parachute is like **wave-making resistance** that grows explosively with speed. To push yourself forward at 60 km/h, you would need a motorcycle engine (massive **installed power**) AND a propeller-like spinning device (a **propulsor**) that "bites" the air and pulls you, rather than just pushing the bicycle pedals.

A high-speed craft behaves exactly like this:
- The **air-water interface** creates wave drag that grows cubically with speed ($R \propto V^3$ to $V^5$).
- Conventional submerged propellers **cavitate** and lose thrust at high speeds.
- Hence, we need **specialized propulsors** (waterjets, supercavitating propellers) and **high power-density engines** (gas turbines, high-speed diesels).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant for High-Speed Marine Propulsion**
> The **Power-to-Weight Ratio** of the propulsion plant is the defining metric. A planing boat typically requires **3–10 kW/tonne**, a hydrofoil **15–30 kW/tonne**, and a surface-effect ship (SES) **20–40 kW/tonne** of displacement. By contrast, a cargo ship needs only **0.5–1.5 kW/tonne**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — POWERING COMPONENTS

## 2.1 The Power Chain in a High-Speed Craft

The total installed power is built up from a chain of efficiencies, each one a potential place to lose marks in a KTU exam if you forget to apply it:

```
Engine (Fuel Power)  →  Shaft Power  →  Delivered Power (DP)
        ↓ η_engine         ↓ η_gearbox      ↓ η_hydrodynamic
    Brake Power (BP)   →  Shaft Power    →  Effective Power (EP)
                                                 ↑
                                        EHP = R_T × V / 75   (metric HP)
```

## 2.2 Resistance Components at High Speed

The total calm-water resistance $R_T$ of a high-speed craft has four principal contributors:

1. **Frictional Resistance ($R_F$)** — Always present; scales with $V^{1.83}$ via the ITTC-78 correlation.
2. **Wave-Making Resistance ($R_W$)** — Dominant at moderate $F_n$; scales with $V^4$ to $V^6$.
3. **Spray Resistance ($R_{SP}$)** — Unique to planing craft; the kinetic energy lost in sheets of water thrown sideways off the hull bottom. Becomes dominant at planing speeds.
4. **Aerodynamic Resistance ($R_A$)** — Air drag on the superstructure; scales with $V^2$. Often 5–15% of total at high speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At planing speeds, **Spray Resistance exceeds Wave Resistance** — this is the single most important fact to remember for KTU 14-mark derivations on Savitsky's method.

## 2.3 EHP → DHP → SHP → BHP Conversion Chain

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Equation |
|----------|--------|------------|----------|
| Effective Horsepower | EHP | Power required to overcome resistance | $EHP = R_T \cdot V / 75$ (in HP, R in kgf, V in m/s) |
| Delivered Horsepower | DHP | Power actually reaching the propeller | $DHP = EHP / \eta_D$, where $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S$ |
| Shaft Horsepower | SHP | Power at the shaft coupling | $SHP \approx DHP$ (assumes shaft losses negligible) |
| Brake Horsepower | BHP | Power measured at engine output | $BHP = SHP / \eta_{GB}$, where $\eta_{GB}$ = gearbox efficiency (≈ 0.97) |
| Installed Power | $P_I$ | Total nameplate power | $P_I = BHP \times (1 + SM)$ |

Where:
- $\eta_H$ = Hull efficiency (0.95–1.10; can be >1 for over-propelled hulls)
- $\eta_R$ = Relative rotative efficiency (0.95–1.05)
- $\eta_S$ = Shaft efficiency (≈ 0.99)
- $\eta_{GB}$ = Gearbox efficiency (0.95–0.98)
- $SM$ = Service Margin (typically 15–30% for high-speed craft)

## 2.4 Propulsion Systems — The KTU Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate**: Students must be able to recommend a propulsor based on the craft type and speed regime. The decision matrix is as follows:

| Craft Type | Speed Regime | Recommended Propulsor | Reason |
|------------|--------------|----------------------|--------|
| Planing monohull (< 40 knots) | $F_n$ < 1.0 | Conventional submerged propeller | Cost-effective, good efficiency |
| Planing monohull (40–50 knots) | $F_n$ ≈ 1.0–1.3 | Surface-piercing propeller | Operates at air-water interface, avoids cavitation |
| Fast ferry / SWATH (35–50 knots) | $F_n$ ≈ 1.0–1.3 | **Waterjet** | No appendage drag, handles shallow water |
| Hydrofoil (40–60 knots) | $F_n$ > 1.3 | Supercavitating propeller OR waterjet | Avoids cavitation at depth |
| ACV / SES (50–80+ knots) | $F_n$ > 2.0 | Air propeller / ducted fan | Operates in air, no cavitation |
| Military patrol craft (60+ knots) | $F_n$ > 1.5 | Supercavitating propeller | Maximum thrust density |

### 2.5 The Three "Why" Steps Behind Propulsor Selection

**Why does a propeller cavitate at high speed?**
The local static pressure on the suction side of the blade drops below the water's vapor pressure. Cavitation erodes blades, vibrates the hull, and limits thrust (thrust breakdown).

**Why does a waterjet outperform a propeller above 35 knots?**
A waterjet ingests water through an intake, accelerates it through an impeller inside a duct, and discharges it through a nozzle. Because the impeller operates inside a duct at positive pressure, it can run at higher RPMs without cavitation, and there are no appendages protruding into the water (no strut/hub drag).

**Why are gas turbines favored over diesels for very high-speed craft?**
Gas turbines have a **power-to-weight ratio of ~3–6 kW/kg** versus ~0.5–1 kW/kg for marine diesels. At speeds above ~50 knots, the required power becomes so large that diesel weight becomes prohibitive.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $EHP = R_T \cdot V / 75$ | Effective Horsepower | $R$ in kgf, $V$ in m/s → HP |
| 2 | $DHP = EHP / \eta_D$ | Delivered Horsepower | $\eta_D = \eta_H \eta_R \eta_S$ |
| 3 | $BHP = DHP / \eta_{GB}$ | Brake Horsepower (engine output) | $\eta_{GB} \approx 0.97$ |
| 4 | $P_I = BHP \times (1 + SM)$ | Installed Power | $SM$ typically **0.20–0.30** |
| 5 | $P/E = \nabla^{2/3} \cdot V^3 / C$ | Admiralty Coefficient | For statistical powering |
| 6 | $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$ | Propulsive efficiency decomposition | $\eta_O = \eta_R \cdot \eta_S \cdot \eta_P$ |
| 7 | $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ | Froude Number | $L$ = waterline length |
| 8 | $T = K_T \rho n^2 D^4$ | Propeller thrust (open water) | $K_T$ from $K_T$–$K_Q$ charts |
| 9 | $Q = K_Q \rho n^2 D^5$ | Propeller torque | $K_Q$ from charts |
| 10 | $\sigma = (p_0 - p_v) / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho n^2 D^2)$ | Cavitation number | Lower $\sigma$ → more cavitation |
| 11 | $R_{SP} / \Delta = C_{SP} \cdot V^{1.5} / \Delta^{0.5}$ | Savitsky spray resistance (semi-empirical) | Planing craft only |
| 12 | $C_{V/\sqrt{L}} = V / \sqrt{L}$ | Speed-length ratio | Used in Savitsky charts |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS — KTU WORKED PROBLEMS

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Horsepower (EHP) from Total Resistance

**Statement:** Show that the effective horsepower required to tow a craft at speed $V$ against resistance $R_T$ is given by $EHP = R_T V / 75$.

**Step 1 — Define mechanical work done per unit time.**
Power is defined as work done per unit time:
$$P = \frac{dW}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Express the work done against resistance.**
If the craft moves a small distance $dx$ against a constant resistance $R_T$, the work done by the towing force is:
$$dW = R_T \cdot dx$$

**Step 3 — Divide by time to get power.**
$$P = \frac{R_T \cdot dx}{dt} = R_T \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} = R_T \cdot V$$

**Step 4 — Convert units to Horsepower.**
By definition, **1 metric horsepower = 75 kgf·m/s** (in gravitational metric units). To convert:
$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$$

where $R_T$ is in **kgf**, $V$ is in **m/s**, and the result is in **metric HP** (1 HP = 735.5 W).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Full Powering Chain

> **Problem (Model KTU 14-Mark Question):**
> A planing craft of displacement $\Delta = 15$ tonnes has a total resistance $R_T = 4500$ kgf at design speed $V = 25$ m/s. The propulsive efficiency is $\eta_D = 0.62$, gearbox efficiency $\eta_{GB} = 0.97$, and a service margin of **25%** is to be applied. Determine:
> (a) Effective Horsepower (EHP)
> (b) Delivered Horsepower (DHP)
> (c) Brake Horsepower (BHP)
> (d) Installed Power $P_I$

### Solution (a) — Effective Horsepower

$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$$

Substituting $R_T = 4500$ kgf and $V = 25$ m/s:

$$EHP = \frac{4500 \times 25}{75}$$

$$EHP = \frac{112500}{75}$$

$$\boxed{EHP = 1500 \text{ HP}}$$

### Solution (b) — Delivered Horsepower

$$DHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{1500}{0.62}$$

$$\boxed{DHP \approx 2419.35 \text{ HP}}$$

### Solution (c) — Brake Horsepower

$$BHP = \frac{DHP}{\eta_{GB}} = \frac{2419.35}{0.97}$$

$$\boxed{BHP \approx 2494.18 \text{ HP}}$$

### Solution (d) — Installed Power

$$P_I = BHP \times (1 + SM) = 2494.18 \times (1 + 0.25)$$

$$P_I = 2494.18 \times 1.25$$

$$\boxed{P_I \approx 3117.7 \text{ HP} \approx 2.29 \text{ MW}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Admiralty Coefficient Form

**Statement:** Derive the Admiralty Coefficient $C_A$ and show its use in powering estimation.

The Admiralty Coefficient relates displacement, speed, and power through a single constant for a class of similar ships:

$$C_A = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{P}$$

**Step 1 — Empirical basis.**
From model tests and full-scale data on similar vessels, the product $\Delta^{2/3} V^3$ is approximately proportional to EHP for a given hull form.

**Step 2 — Rearranging for EHP:**
$$EHP = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{C_A}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to DHP using overall efficiency $\eta_D$:**
$$DHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3}{C_A \cdot \eta_D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important for KTU**: For high-speed craft, $C_A$ is typically in the range **80–150** (for planing craft, lower values around 80–110; for hydrofoils, higher values 130–180 because of lower wave drag).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Cavitation Number Check

> **Problem:** A surface-piercing propeller of diameter $D = 0.8$ m rotates at $n = 1500$ RPM in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³) at depth $h = 0.3$ m. Atmospheric pressure $p_a = 101325$ Pa, vapor pressure $p_v = 2350$ Pa. Calculate the cavitation number.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Tip speed calculation.**
$$V_{tip} = \pi D n = \pi \times 0.8 \times \frac{1500}{60} = \pi \times 0.8 \times 25$$

$$V_{tip} \approx 62.83 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Local static pressure at blade depth.**
$$p_0 = p_a + \rho g h = 101325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.3$$

$$p_0 = 101325 + 3016.6 \approx 104341.6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Cavitation number formula:**
$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{tip}^2}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{104341.6 - 2350}{0.5 \times 1025 \times (62.83)^2}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{101991.6}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 3947.6}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{101991.6}{2023145}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 0.050}$$

**Interpretation:** A cavitation number of **0.05** is very low — the propeller is operating well into the cavitation regime, justifying the use of a **surface-piercing** design (which ventilates rather than cavitates destructively).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Propulsor Selection Logic

| Speed Range (knots) | $F_n$ Range | Cavitation Risk | Recommended Propulsor | Typical Engine |
|---------------------|-------------|------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| 0–25 | < 0.45 | Low | Conventional propeller | Diesel |
| 25–40 | 0.45–1.0 | Moderate | Conventional propeller with tip clearance | Diesel |
| 40–55 | 1.0–1.5 | High | **Waterjet** / Surface-piercing | High-speed diesel / Gas turbine |
| 55–80 | 1.5–2.5 | Very high | **Supercavitating propeller** / Waterjet | Gas turbine |
| > 80 | > 2.5 | Extreme | **Air propeller / Ducted fan** | Gas turbine (marinized) |

---

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Powering Chain Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Chemical Energy] --> B[Engine Block]
    B -->|η_engine| C[Brake Power BHP]

    F -->|η_propulsive| G[Effective Power EHP]

    L2[Gearbox Friction]

    C -.-> L2
    D -.-> L3
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define "Effective Horsepower" (EHP) and "Delivered Horsepower" (DHP). Establish the relationship between them with a clearly labeled formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **EHP (1 mark):** The effective horsepower is the power required to overcome the total resistance of the vessel and move it at the design speed through calm water. Mathematically, $EHP = R_T \times V / 75$ (where $R_T$ is in kgf and $V$ is in m/s).
- **DHP (1 mark):** The delivered horsepower is the actual power delivered to the propeller by the engine after accounting for transmission losses.
- **Relationship (1 mark):**
$$DHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{EHP}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S}$$
where $\eta_D$ is the propulsive efficiency combining hull efficiency $\eta_H$, relative rotative efficiency $\eta_R$, and shaft efficiency $\eta_S$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Why are waterjets preferred over conventional propellers for fast ferries operating at 40+ knots?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **No cavitation at high tip speeds (1 mark):** Waterjet impellers operate inside a duct at positive static pressure, allowing higher rotational speeds without cavitation, which would otherwise destroy blades and limit thrust.
2. **No appendage drag (1 mark):** Unlike a submerged propeller with shaft, strut, and rudder, a waterjet has no appendages projecting into the water, eliminating parasitic drag.
3. **Safety and maneuverability (1 mark):** The intake is recessed, safe for swimmers; thrust vectoring via the reversing bucket enables rapid crash-stop and tight turns.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the components of a typical **waterjet propulsion system** used in modern fast ferries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A planing craft of displacement **$\Delta = 12$ tonnes** is to operate at **$V = 22$ m/s**. Its total resistance at this speed is **$R_T = 3200$ kgf**. Given propulsive efficiency $\eta_D = 0.60$, gearbox efficiency $\eta_{GB} = 0.97$, and service margin $SM = 0.25$, calculate the **installed engine power** in kW. State one justification for selecting a waterjet over a conventional propeller for this craft. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to A(a): Waterjet Components**

A waterjet propulsion system consists of the following sequential components (describe each for 1 mark):

1. **Intake (Duct):** A carefully profiled duct at the bottom of the hull that ingests water with minimum loss. The intake lip is designed to avoid ventilation and cavitation inception.

2. **Impeller (Pump):** A multi-blade axial or mixed-flow rotor driven via an input shaft from the gearbox. The impeller adds kinetic energy to the water. Typical impeller diameters: 0.5–1.5 m for fast ferries.

3. **Stator (Guide Vanes):** A row of stationary vanes directly downstream of the impeller that removes the rotational (swirl) component, converting rotational energy into linear momentum and increasing efficiency by 5–10%.

4. **Nozzle (Steering Nozzle):** A variable-area convergent nozzle that converts the high-velocity flow into thrust by reducing velocity and increasing pressure. The nozzle is often rotatable to provide steering without a rudder.

5. **Reverse Bucket (Astern Bucket):** A deflector that pivots across the jet exit to redirect forward thrust rearward, providing astern propulsion without reversing the impeller rotation.

6. **Input Shaft Seal & Bearings:** Lubricated bearings and seals to handle the axial and radial loads from the impeller.

**Sketch (descriptive flow):**
```
       Water Flow →→→
    ┌──────────────────────┐
    │      INTAKE DUCT      │
    └──────────┬───────────┘
               ▼
    ┌──────────────────────┐
    │   IMPELLER (rotates)  │
    └──────────┬───────────┘
               ▼
    ┌──────────────────────┐
    │   STATOR (stationary) │
    └──────────┬───────────┘
               ▼
    ┌──────────────────────┐
    │  STEERING NOZZLE →→→ THRUST
    └──────────┬───────────┘
               ▼
        ┌─────────────┐
        │  ATERN BUCKET  │  (deploys for reverse)
        └─────────────┘
```

---

#### **Solution to A(b): Installed Power Calculation**

**Step 1 — Effective Horsepower (EHP):**
$$EHP = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{75} = \frac{3200 \times 22}{75}$$

$$EHP = \frac{70400}{75} \approx 938.67 \text{ HP}$$

**Step 2 — Delivered Horsepower (DHP):**
$$DHP = \frac{EHP}{\eta_D} = \frac{938.67}{0.60} \approx 1564.45 \text{ HP}$$

**Step 3 — Brake Horsepower (BHP):**
$$BHP = \frac{DHP}{\eta_{GB}} = \frac{1564.45}{0.97} \approx 1612.84 \text{ HP}$$

**Step 4 — Installed Power (in HP):**
$$P_I = BHP \times (1 + SM) = 1612.84 \times 1.25 \approx 2016.05 \text{ HP}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kW** (1 HP = 0.7355 kW):
$$P_I = 2016.05 \times 0.7355 \approx \boxed{1482.8 \text{ kW}}$$

**Justification for Waterjet (1 mark):**
At $V = 22$ m/s ≈ **42.8 knots**, the craft is operating in the **semi-planing / planing regime** where conventional propellers begin to suffer severe cavitation due to the high local velocities at blade tips. A waterjet avoids this cavitation problem because the impeller is enclosed in a duct operating at above-ambient static pressure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – Model]**

**(a)** Compare **conventional submerged propellers, surface-piercing propellers, supercavitating propellers, and waterjets** as propulsors for high-speed craft. Present your answer in a tabular form covering: principle of operation, typical speed range, cavitation behavior, efficiency, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A supercavitating propeller of diameter **$D = 0.6$ m** rotates at **$n = 1800$ RPM** in seawater at depth **$h = 0.4$ m**. Compute the cavitation number and comment on whether supercavitation is achieved. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $p_a = 101325$ Pa, $p_v = 2350$ Pa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B(a): Comparative Table**

| Feature | Conventional Submerged | Surface-Piercing | Supercavitating | Waterjet |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|------------------|----------|
| **Principle** | Blade operates fully submerged, thrust from pressure differential on suction & pressure sides | Blade rotates at air-water interface; operates partly in air | Blade deliberately generates a single large vapor cavity covering suction side | Accelerates water through internal duct; impeller enclosed |
| **Speed Range (knots)** | 0–35 | 25–50 | 35–70+ | 25–60+ |
| **Cavitation Behavior** | Cavitates above ~30 kn; limits thrust | Ventilates (controlled air ingestion) instead of cavitating | Designed to operate inside a stable vapor cavity | Impeller inside pressurized duct — minimal cavitation |
| **Efficiency** | Highest in calm-water low-speed regime (0.65–0.75) | Moderate (0.55–0.65) | Lower (0.45–0.60) due to single-sided thrust | High (0.65–0.75) at design speed |
| **Typical Application** | Tugboats, displacement craft, slow ferries | Planing fishing boats, small fast craft | Hydrofoils, military patrol boats | Fast ferries, SWATH, military vessels |
| **Drawback** | Cavitation erosion & vibration | Spray ingestion, asymmetric thrust | Unbalanced side-force, noise | Cost, complex duct geometry |

*(1 mark per major row of insight; full marks for clear comparison)*

---

#### **Solution to B(b): Cavitation Number Computation**

**Step 1 — Tip speed:**
$$V_{tip} = \pi D n = \pi \times 0.6 \times \frac{1800}{60}$$

$$V_{tip} = \pi \times 0.6 \times 30 = 18\pi \approx 56.55 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Local static pressure at blade depth:**
$$p_0 = p_a + \rho g h = 101325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 0.4$$

$$p_0 = 101325 + 4021.74 = 105346.74 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Cavitation number:**
$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{tip}^2} = \frac{105346.74 - 2350}{0.5 \times 1025 \times (56.55)^2}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{102996.74}{0.5 \times 1025 \times 3197.9}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{102996.74}{1638925}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 0.0628}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on supercavitation (2 marks):**
A cavitation number $\sigma \approx 0.06$ is **well below the critical threshold of $\sigma_{cr} \approx 0.10$–$0.20$** for the onset of developed cavitation on conventional propellers. This low value confirms that the local pressures on the blade suction side are below vapor pressure across nearly the entire blade span, producing the **supercavitating regime** — a single, stable vapor cavity enveloping the suction side, which is the **design intent** for this propulsor.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the 75 divisor:** When using metric units (kgf, m/s), the constant is **75**, not 75 × 9.81. Marks are deducted if students confuse with SI (W) conversion.
> 2. **Mixing up efficiency hierarchies:** Many students write $DHP = EHP \times \eta_D$. The correct direction is $DHP = EHP / \eta_D$ — more power must be *delivered* than is *effective* because of losses.
> 3. **Ignoring the Service Margin:** Installed power = $BHP \times (1 + SM)$. Skipping the SM loses **1 mark** automatically in any 14-mark question.
> 4. **Cavitating about cavitation:** Students often equate "cavitation" with "no thrust." In reality, partial cavitation is acceptable; **supercavitation is engineered**, not an accident.
> 5. **Confusing waterjet and pump-jet:** A waterjet discharges to the open sea; a pump-jet discharges through a closed duct. Only the former is the standard propulsor for fast craft.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Important Things to Remember (Module 3: Powering & Propulsion)**

### Powering Fundamentals
- ✅ The power chain: **Fuel Power → BHP → SHP → DHP → THP → EHP**, with losses at every step.
- ✅ **EHP** is the "useful" power; everything before it has losses expressed as efficiencies.
- ✅ **Service Margin (15–30%)** is mandatory for installed power sizing.
- ✅ **Admiralty Coefficient** $C_A = \Delta^{2/3} V^3 / P$ is a quick statistical estimator.

### Resistance at High Speed
- ✅ **Spray resistance** dominates planing craft — not wave resistance (a unique reversal of low-speed behavior).
- ✅ **Wave resistance** grows with $V^4$ to $V^6$ at moderate $F_n$.
- ✅ **Air resistance** is small but non-negligible (5–15%) and scales as $V^2$.

### Propulsion System Selection
- ✅ **< 30 knots**: Conventional propeller + diesel.
- ✅ **30–45 knots**: Waterjet or surface-piercing propeller.
- ✅ **45–70 knots**: Supercavitating propeller or waterjet + gas turbine.
- ✅ **> 70 knots**: Air propeller / ducted fan + marinized gas turbine.

### Cavitation
- ✅ **Cavitation number** $\sigma$ ↓ → more cavitation.
- ✅ **Supercavitation** is intentional and engineered for $\sigma < 0.10$.
- ✅ **Ventilation** is surface-piercing's "controlled" version of cavitation.

### Numerical Skills to Master
- ✅ Convert EHP ↔ DHP ↔ BHP ↔ Installed Power in 4 clean steps.
- ✅ Compute cavitation number from $p_0, p_v, \rho, V_{tip}$.
- ✅ Apply the correct efficiency in the correct direction (divide, not multiply).
- ✅ Remember to convert HP to kW: **1 HP = 0.7355 kW** (or **1 kW = 1.36 HP**).

---

> 📚 **End of Module 3 Notes — Powering and Propulsion**
